Autism activist activities do not always look like other forms of political activism. For example, many autistic people cannot participate in in-person public protests due to [[sensory overload]] from the noise.<ref name=":1">{{Citation |last=Reading |first=Anna Amza |title=Remembering Autism in the Memory-Activism Nexus |date=2024-11-18 |work=Dynamics, Mediation, Mobilization |pages=377–382 |editor-last=Erll |editor-first=Astrid |publisher=De Gruyter |doi=10.1515/9783111439273-053 |isbn=978-3-11-143927-3 |editor2-last=Knittel |editor2-first=Susanne |editor3-last=Wüstenberg |editor3-first=Jenny |doi-access=free}}</ref> Other cultural assumptions about the correct way to engage in activism, such as "giving people a voice", are not consistent with valuing [[non-speaking autistic]] people.<ref name=":1" />

=== Autistic Pride Day ===
{{Main|Autistic Pride Day}}

[[Autistic Pride Day]] is an annual pride celebration that was established by [[Aspies For Freedom]] and is observed on 18 June. Organizers of the celebration have compared their efforts to the [[Civil rights movement|civil rights]] and [[LGBTQ movements|LGBTQ social movements]].<ref name=SanerDisease/><ref>{{cite web |url=http://communities.washingtontimes.com/neighborhood/autism-unexpected/2011/nov/1/autistics-speaking-day-broadcasts-autistic-voices/ |title=Autistics Speaking Day broadcasts autistic voices |department=Communities |work=[[The Washington Times]] |date=1 November 2011 |access-date=5 October 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111105020831/http://communities.washingtontimes.com/neighborhood/autism-unexpected/2011/nov/1/autistics-speaking-day-broadcasts-autistic-voices/ |archive-date=5 November 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{citation|title=Autistic Pride Day 2016: Why we are proud to have autism|first=Kashmira|last=Gander|work=The Independent |date=18 June 2016|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/life-style/health-and-families/autistic-pride-day-2016-why-we-are-proud-to-have-autism-neurotypical-a7085066.html|access-date=24 December 2016|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160619140237/http://www.independent.co.uk/life-style/health-and-families/autistic-pride-day-2016-why-we-are-proud-to-have-autism-neurotypical-a7085066.html|archive-date=19 June 2016}}</ref>

=== Autistics Speaking Day ===
Autistics Speaking Day was established by members of the autistic community and is observed annually on 1 November. It provides an opportunity for autistic people to challenge negative stereotypes by speaking for themselves and sharing their stories. The first Autistics Speaking Day was held in 2010.<ref>{{cite news |title=Social networkers switch off for autism awareness |first=Monique |last=Ross |work=[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]] |location=Australia |date=1 November 2010 |url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2010-11-01/social-networkers-switch-off-for-autism-awareness/2319638?section=justin |access-date=24 December 2016 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111111232401/http://www.abc.net.au/news/2010-11-01/social-networkers-switch-off-for-autism-awareness/2319638?section=justin |archive-date=11 November 2011}}</ref> According to Corina Becker, one of the founders, the day is to "acknowledge our difficulties while sharing our strengths, passions, and interests".<ref>{{citation |date=9 September 2011 |title=The Beginnings of Autistic Speaking Day |first=Corina |last=Becker |website=Thinking Person's Guide to Autism |url=http://www.thinkingautismguide.com/2011/09/beginnings-of-autistic-speaking-day.html |access-date=24 December 2016 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130516054253/http://www.thinkingautismguide.com/2011/09/beginnings-of-autistic-speaking-day.html |archive-date=16 May 2013}}</ref> The idea for the event developed out of opposition to a ''[[Communication Shutdown]]'' fundraising campaign led by U.S. charity Autism Speaks. Participants had been asked to "simulate having autism" by straying from all forms of online communication for one day. That event received criticism and accused Autism Speaks of missing the point of what autism actually is, referring to autistic individuals' comfort using other means of communication.<ref>{{citation |first=Emily |last=Willingham |magazine=[[Forbes]] |date=31 October 2015 |title=The Little Black Book of Billionaire Secrets Autistics Speaking Day Is November 1, So It Is (Really) Time To Listen |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/emilywillingham/2015/10/31/autistics-speaking-day-is-november-1-so-it-is-really-time-to-listen/ |access-date=24 December 2016 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151104030414/http://www.forbes.com/sites/emilywillingham/2015/10/31/autistics-speaking-day-is-november-1-so-it-is-really-time-to-listen/#ecd11fb77ec3 |archive-date=4 November 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://communities.washingtontimes.com/neighborhood/autism-unexpected/2011/nov/1/autistics-speaking-day-broadcasts-autistic-voices/ |title=Autistics Speaking Day broadcasts autistic voices |website=The Washington Times |department=Communities |format= Blog |last1=Winegardner |first1=Jean |date=1 November 2011 |access-date=5 October 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121105201059/http://communities.washingtontimes.com/neighborhood/autism-unexpected/2011/nov/1/autistics-speaking-day-broadcasts-autistic-voices |archive-date=5 November 2012|url-status=dead}}</ref>

=== Autism Acceptance Day ===
In 2011, the first Autism Acceptance Day celebrations were organized by Paula Durbin Westby as a response to traditional "autism awareness" campaigns many within the autistic community found harmful and insufficient.<ref name="autismacceptancemonth1">{{cite web |author=Administrator |url=http://www.autismacceptancemonth.com/acceptance-month-conclusion/ |title=is coming to a close. Thank you for your support! |publisher=Autism Acceptance Month |date=30 April 2013 |access-date=5 October 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130504082430/http://www.autismacceptancemonth.com/acceptance-month-conclusion/ |archive-date=4 May 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.autismacceptancemonth.com/about/ |title=About |publisher=Autism Acceptance Month |date=18 April 2013 |access-date=5 October 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130405002954/http://www.autismacceptancemonth.com/about/ |archive-date=5 April 2013}}</ref> Autism Acceptance Day is now held every April.<ref name="autismacceptancemonth1"/> Awareness focuses on informing others of the existence of autism, while acceptance pushes towards validating and honoring the autism community. By providing tools and educational material, people are encouraged to embrace the challenges autistic people face and celebrate their strengths. Instead of encouraging people to wear blue, Autism Acceptance Day encourages people to wear red.<ref>{{Cite news |title=April Is Autism Acceptance Month |url=https://www.huffingtonpost.com/laura-jo/april-is-autism-acceptance-month_b_7004260.html |work=[[HuffPost]] |access-date=26 October 2015 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150409070622/http://www.huffingtonpost.com/laura-jo/april-is-autism-acceptance-month_b_7004260.html |archive-date=9 April 2015 |date=6 April 2015}}</ref>

=== Disability Day of Mourning ===
In 2012, autistic activist Zoe Gross organized the first [[Disability Day of Mourning]] vigil held in memory of people with disabilities murdered by family members or caregivers.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2013-08-15 |title=Day of Mourning: Affirming the Value of Disabled People's Lives |url=https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/blog/2013/08/15/day-mourning-affirming-value-disabled-people-s-lives |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180517223112/https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/blog/2013/08/15/day-mourning-affirming-value-disabled-people-s-lives |archive-date=17 May 2018 |access-date=2018-05-17 |publisher=White House |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=27 February 2018 |title=Disability Day of Mourning vigil to be held at Schine Student Center |url=http://dailyorange.com/2018/02/disability-day-mourning-vigil-held-schine-student-center/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180517223347/http://dailyorange.com/2018/02/disability-day-mourning-vigil-held-schine-student-center/ |archive-date=17 May 2018 |access-date=2018-05-17 |newspaper=The Daily Orange – The Independent Student Newspaper |location=Syracuse University |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="AsanMourning">{{Cite web |last=Network |first=Autistic Self Advocacy |title=ASAN Day of Mourning on March 1st{{!}} Autistic Self Advocacy Network |url=http://autisticadvocacy.org/2013/02/asan-day-of-mourning-on-march-1st/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130505200153/http://autisticadvocacy.org/2013/02/asan-day-of-mourning-on-march-1st/ |archive-date=2013-05-05 |access-date=2018-05-17 |website=autisticadvocacy.org |language=en-US}}</ref> These vigils are now held annually on 1 March globally, often by local [[self-advocacy]] and [[Disability rights movement|disability rights]] groups.<ref name="AsanMourning" />

=== #RedInstead ===
In 2015, autistic activist Alanna Rose Whitney created the social media campaign #WalkInRed, later rebranded #RedInstead to be more inclusive of wheelchair users, as another alternative to Light It Up Blue.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Somashekhar |first=Sandhya |date=2015-07-20 |title=How autistic adults banded together to start a movement |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/the-next-civil-rights-movement-accepting-adults-with-autism/2015/07/20/e7c6743e-f338-11e4-bcc4-e8141e5eb0c9_story.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180517153411/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/the-next-civil-rights-movement-accepting-adults-with-autism/2015/07/20/e7c6743e-f338-11e4-bcc4-e8141e5eb0c9_story.html |archive-date=17 May 2018 |access-date=2018-05-17 |newspaper=The Washington Post |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2018-04-23 |title=Human Rights Advocate |url=http://alannarosewhitney.com/human-rights-advocate/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180517154007/http://alannarosewhitney.com/human-rights-advocate/ |archive-date=17 May 2018 |access-date=2018-05-17 |language=en-US}}</ref>

==Scholarship==
Autism received increasing attention from [[Social science|social-science]] scholars in the early 2000s, with the goals of improving support services and therapies, arguing that autism should be tolerated as a difference not a disorder, and by how autism affects the definition of personhood and identity.<ref name=Silverman>{{cite journal |journal=BioSocieties |year=2008 |volume=3 |issue=3 |pages=325–341 |title=Fieldwork on another planet: social science perspectives on the autism spectrum |author= Silverman C |doi=10.1017/S1745855208006236 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Sociological research has also investigated how social institutions, particularly families, cope with the challenges associated with autism.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=Contexts |year=2009 |volume=8 |issue=2 |pages=40–45 |title=Autism, through a social lens |last=Poulson |first=Stephen |doi=10.1525/ctx.2009.8.2.40 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

A study published on 20 January 2021, by the [[University of Texas at Dallas]] suggests that educating non-autistic people about the strengths and challenges of autistic people can help reduce stigma and misconceptions surrounding autism, which may help increase social inclusion of autistic people.<ref name=":0">{{cite press release |title=Reducing biases about autism may increase social inclusion, study finds |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2021/02/210208085441.htm |work=ScienceDaily |publisher=[[University of Texas at Dallas]] |date=8 February 2021}}</ref> The study also found that [[Implicit stereotype|implicit biases]] about autism were harder to overcome for non-autistic people.<ref name=":0" />
