2025|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> and [[Bactria]],<ref>{{Cite book|title=United Arab Emirates: a new perspective|date=2001|publisher=Trident Press|others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter.|isbn=978-1900724470|location=London|oclc=47140175}}</ref> and the Levant.<ref>{{Citation |title=Bronze Age Reed Boats of Magan and Magillum Boats of Meluḫḫa in Cuneiform Literature |date=2018-11-09 |url=https://brill.com/view/book/edcoll/9789004362321/BP000017.xml |work=Stories of Globalisation: The Red Sea and the Persian Gulf from Late Prehistory to Early Modernity |pages=139 |editor-last=Manzo |editor-first=Andrea |access-date=2023-08-17 |publisher=BRILL |doi=10.1163/9789004362321_009 |isbn=978-90-04-36232-1 |s2cid=198516354 |editor2-last=Zazzaro |editor2-first=Chiara |editor3-last=De Falco |editor3-first=Diana Joyce|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Through the [[Iron Age in the United Arab Emirates|three defined Iron Ages]] and the subsequent Hellenistic period, the area was an important coastal trading [[entrepôt]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ancient Cities of the Emirates |url=http://www.beyonddubai.net/ancient-cities-of-the-emirates.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171026111746/http://www.beyonddubai.net/ancient-cities-of-the-emirates.html |archive-date=2017-10-26 |access-date=2017-10-26 |website=Beyond Dubai: Seeking Lost Cities in the Emirates by David Millar}}</ref> It was [[Islamization|Islamised]] in the 7th century, when it again emerged as an important centre for trade, particularly around the ports of [[Julfar]], [[Dibba Al-Hisn|Dibba]] and [[Khor Fakkan]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=Power |first=Timothy |title=The UAE in Early Islamic History |url=https://www.academia.edu/7827484}}</ref> Linked to the Eastern Arab trading network that centred around the [[Ormus|Kingdom of Hormuz]], these ports formed an important link in the Arab monopoly of trade between the East and Europe.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Reid |first=Struan |title=The silk and spice routes, cultures and civilizations |date=1994 |publisher=Belitha [u.a.] |others=UNESCO |isbn=978-1-85561-265-5 |series=UNESCO integral study of the silk roads |location=London}}</ref>

In the early years of the 16th century, the [[Portuguese people|Portuguese]] under [[Afonso de Albuquerque]] invaded the area and disrupted the Arab Eastern trade networks, triggering a decline in the Arab dominance of the Eastern trade networks and a rise in regional conflict. Conflicts between the maritime communities of the Trucial Coast and the British led to the sacking of [[Ras Al Khaimah]] by [[British Empire|British]] forces, which resulted in the first of several British treaties with the coastal rulers in 1820 (leading to the adoption of the name the [[Trucial States]])<ref>Balfour-Paul, G., ''The End of Empire in the Middle East: Britain's Relinquishment of Power in her Last Three Arab Dependencies'', Cambridge University Press, 1984, {{ISBN|978-0-521-46636-3}}</ref> and their status as a British semi-protectorate.

An early-1968 British decision to withdraw from its involvement in the Trucial States led to the decision to form a federation between two of the most influential Trucial rulers, [[Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan|Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan]] of Abu Dhabi and [[Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum]] of Dubai, who shook hands on 18 February 1968 in a desert tent between their emirates. They met with the rulers of the other five Trucial States, together with Bahrain and Qatar, to discuss a federation on 25 February, and in a 27 February joint announcement named the intended federation The Federation of the Arab Emirates.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=359–360}}</ref>

The United Arab Emirates achieved independence from Britain on 2 December 1971. Six of the seven [[Emirates of the United Arab Emirates|emirate]]s ([[Emirate of Abu Dhabi|Abu Dhabi]], [[Emirate of Dubai|Dubai]], [[Emirate of Sharjah|Sharjah]], [[Emirate of Ajman|Ajman]], [[Umm Al Quwain]] and [[Fujairah]]) declared their union that day and the seventh, Ras Al Khaimah, joined the federation on 10 February 1972.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-12-07 |title=How was the UAE founded? |url=https://www.euronews.com/2018/12/07/how-was-the-uae-founded |access-date=2023-08-17 |website=euronews |language=en}}</ref>

== Prehistory ==
The area known today as the United Arab Emirates has yielded evidence of human habitation dating back 125,000 years and formed one of the routes of human dispersal from Africa to populate the world.<ref name=":02">{{cite journal |last1=Armitage|first1=Simon|date=28 January 2011|title=The Southern Route "Out of Africa": Evidence for an Early Expansion of Modern Humans into Arabia|journal=Science|volume=331|issue=6016|pages=453–456|doi=10.1126/science.1199113|pmid=21273486|author2=Sabah A. Jasim|author3=Anthony E. Marks|author4=Adrian G. Parker|author5=Vitaly I. Usik|author6=Hans-Peter Uerpmann|bibcode=2011Sci...331..453A|s2cid=20296624}}</ref> Its archaeology attests to periods of Paleolithic, Neolithic, Bronze Age and Iron Age occupation, prior to the Pre-Islamic Recent ([[Pre-Islamic recent period|PIR]]) and Islamic era.

=== Dispersal from Africa ===
Early hominid occupation in the UAE is attested by finds of knapped flint tools among extensive assemblages found at Jebel Barakah in [[Abu Dhabi]] yielding a date of 200,000 BCE.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2009-09-18 |title=Actualité {{!}} The oldest sanctuary in Arabia is discovered: a dugong bone mound on Akab Island |url=https://www.inrap.fr/en/oldest-sanctuary-arabia-discovered-dugong-bone-mound-akab-island-12146 |access-date=2025-08-01 |website=Inrap |language=en}}</ref> The earliest evidence for the occupation of the area today known as the UAE by anatomically modern humans, ''[[Human|Homo Sapiens]],'' is found at [[Jebel Faya]], where in 2011 archaeologists found a lithic assemblage including hand-axes and several kinds of scrapers and perforators. These objects resemble the tools used by [[early modern humans]] in [[East Africa]]. According to [[thermoluminescence dating]], the artefacts are 125,000 years old.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=31}}</ref> 

The find at Faya represents some of the earliest evidence of modern humanity outside Africa, indicating that humans left Africa much earlier than previously thought.<ref name="Early human may have left Africa earlier | Human World | EarthSky-2011">{{Cite web|url=http://earthsky.org/human-world/new-timeline-for-first-early-human-exodus-out-of-africa|title=Early human may have left Africa earlier | Human World | EarthSky|date=February 2011|access-date=2016-10-27|archive-date=2016-03-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304192921/http://earthsky.org/human-world/new-timeline-for-first-early-human-exodus-out-of-africa|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="BBC 01-2018">{{Cite news |last=Ghosh |first=Pallab |year=2018 |title=Modern humans left Africa much earlier |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-42817323 |access-date=2018-01-28 |work=BBC News |language=en-GB}}</ref> The site, today a UNESCO World Heritage Site, has been preserved with discoveries related to later cultures, including tombs and other finds from the [[Hafit period|Hafit]], [[Umm Al Nar culture|Umm Al Nar]], [[Wadi Suq culture|Wadi Suq]], [[Iron Age in the United Arab Emirates|Iron Age]], Hellenistic and Islamic periods on display at Sharjah's [[Mleiha Archaeological Centre]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=32}}</ref>

===Neolithic===
{{main|Neolithic in the United Arab Emirates}}

Eastern Arabia is thought to have been uninhabitable during the [[Last Glacial Maximum|glacial maximum period]], from 68,000 to 8000 BCE. Finds from the Stone Age Arabian [[Hand axe|Bifacial]] and [[Ubaid period|Ubaid]] cultures include knapped stone arrow and axe heads and Ubaid pottery and are evidence of human habitation in the area from 5000 to 3100 BCE, and demonstrate a clear linkage between the early settlements of the Gulf and those of Mesopotamia.<ref name="McNabb-2025d" /> 

The inland [[necropolis]] at [[Jebel Buhais]] in [[Emirate of Sharjah|Sharjah]] is the oldest burial site in the Emirates, with burials as old as the 5th millennium BCE. The archaeological record indicates that the Neolithic Arabian Bifacial/Ubaid period abruptly ended in eastern Arabia and the [[Oman]] peninsula at 3800 BCE, following a period of aridity, lowered lake levels and the accretion of widespread sand dune fields.<ref name="Parker2006">{{cite journal|last=Parker|first=Adrian G.|display-authors=etal|year=2006|title=A record of Holocene climate change from lake geochemical analyses in southeastern Arabia|url=http://www.gulfnexus.org/articles/geo/2006a%20Parker%20et%20al.pdf|url-status=dead|journal=Quaternary Research|volume=66|issue=3|pages=465–476|doi=10.1016/j.yqres.2006.07.001|bibcode=2006QuRes..66..465P|s2cid=140158532|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080910214744/http://www.gulfnexus.org/articles/geo/2006a%20Parker%20et%20al.pdf|archive-date=September 10, 2008}}</ref> This extreme arid climate devastated the human population to the area west of the [[Hajar Mountains]],<ref name="McNabb-2025d" /> leading to the [[Dark Millennium (United Arab Emirates)|Dark Millennium]]: a fallow period in the human archaeological record, likely triggered by climate change<ref name="Uerpmann2002" /> and covering much of the period between 4000 and 3200 BCE.<ref name="McNabb-2025d" /> Rare exceptions to the abandonment of the west coast include finds in the [[Baynunah Formation]] of the [[Al Dhafra, Abu Dhabi|western]] Emirate of Abu Dhabi, where a [[camel]]-slaughter site was discovered dating to that period,<ref name="DCTAD2017" /> as well as a distinctive mound of dugong bones found on [[Akab Island]] in Umm Al Quwain dated to 3500 and 3200 BCE.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2009-09-18 |title=Actualité {{!}} The oldest sanctuary in Arabia is discovered: a dugong bone mound on Akab Island |url=https://www.inrap.fr/en/oldest-sanctuary-arabia-discovered-dugong-bone-mound-akab-island-12146 |access-date=2025-08-01 |website=Inrap |language=en}}</ref>

===Hafit period===
{{main|Hafit period}}

The [[Hafit period]] followed the Dark Millennium as the re-emergence of human habitation on the western coast of the Emirates.<ref name="Uerpmann2002">{{cite book|last=Uerpmann|first=M.|title=Archaeology of the United Arab Emirates|publisher=Trident Press|year=2002|isbn=978-1-900724-88-3|editor=Potts, D.|series=Proceedings of the First International Conference on the Archaeology of the U.A.E.|location=London|pages=74–81|chapter=The Dark Millennium—Remarks on the final Stone Age in the Emirates and Oman|editor2=al-Naboodah, H.|editor3=Hellyer, P.}}</ref> The Hafit period was named after finds of over 300 burials of distinctive beehive-shaped tombs in the mountainous area of [[Jebel Hafeet|Jebel Hafit]] in the [[Al Ain Region]].<ref name="Morton2016">{{cite book|last1=Morton|first1=Michael Quentin|title=Keepers of the Golden Shore: A History of the United Arab Emirates|date=15 April 2016|publisher=Reaktion Books|location=London|isbn=978-1780235806|pages=178–199|edition=1st|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-oxfDQAAQBAJ|access-date=8 November 2016|archive-date=19 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230219202704/https://books.google.com/books?id=-oxfDQAAQBAJ|url-status=live}}</ref> The period defines early Bronze Age human settlement in the United Arab Emirates and Oman as from 3,200 to 2,600 BCE. Hafit period tombs and remains have also been found across the UAE and Oman at sites such as [[Bidaa Bint Saud]],<ref>{{Cite news |last=Salama |first=Samir |date=2011-12-30 |title=Al Ain bears evidence of a culture's ability to adapt |url=https://gulfnews.com/news/uae/culture/al-ain-bears-evidence-of-a-culture-s-ability-to-adapt-1.958876 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180716165606/https://gulfnews.com/news/uae/culture/al-ain-bears-evidence-of-a-culture-s-ability-to-adapt-1.958876 |archive-date=2018-07-16 |access-date=2018-07-16 |work=[[Gulf News]]}}</ref> [[Jebel Buhais]] and [[Al Buraimi Governorate|Buraimi]].<ref name="Thornton_al2006">{{cite book |author1=Christopher P. Thornton |title=The Bronze Age Towers at Bat, Sultanate of Oman |author2=Charlotte M. Cable |author3=Gregory L. Possehl |publisher=[[University of Pennsylvania Press]], Inc. |year=2016 |isbn=978-1-9345-3607-0 |pages=i–vi |doi=10.2307/j.ctv2t4ct6.1}}</ref>

Pottery finds at Hafit period sites demonstrate trading links to Mesopotamia, contiguous to the [[Jemdet Nasr period|Jemdat Nasr]] period (3100 – 2900 BCE).<ref name="Magee 20142">{{Citation |last=Magee |first=Peter |title=The Archaeology of Prehistoric Arabia |pages=275–278 |year=2014 |chapter=Adaptation and Social Formation in Ancient Arabia |publisher=Cambridge University Press |doi=10.1017/cbo9781139016667.011 |isbn=9781139016667}}</ref> Finds have shown locally manufactured pottery emerged during the transition between the Hafit and Umm Al Nar periods, approximately 2800 to 2700 BCE<ref name=":1">Typological and Chronological Consideration of the Ceramics at Bat, Oman - Christopher P. Thornton & Royal Omar Ghazal.</ref> It is now thought the transition between the two cultural periods is marked by a decline in links between Southeastern Arabia and Mesopotamia,<ref name=":1" /> a pattern that would be repeated, albeit more emphatically, in the transitional period between the later Umm Al Nar and Wadi Suq cultures.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book|title=The archaeology of prehistoric Arabia : adaptation and social formation from the neolithic to the iron age|author=Magee, Peter|isbn=9780521862318|location=New York|oclc=852824778|date=2014-05-19}}</ref>

==Bronze Age==
{{main|Umm Al Nar culture|Wadi Suq culture}}

[[Umm Al Nar culture|Umm Al Nar]] (also known as Umm an-Nar) was a [[Bronze Age]] culture defined by archaeologists as spanning 2600 to 2000 BCE in the area of the present-day UAE and Oman. The etymology derives from the island of the same name, the first 'find site' of burials from this period, which is adjacent to the city of [[Abu Dhabi]].<ref>[http://www.uaeinteract.com/history/e_walk/con_4/con4_17.asp UAE History: 20,000 — 2,000 years ago - UAEinteract<!-- Bot generated title -->] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130613090939/http://www.uaeinteract.com/history/e_walk/con_4/con4_17.asp |date=2013-06-13 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=UNESCO — Tentative Lists|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5660/|website=Settlement and Cemetery of Umm an-Nar Island|publisher=UNESCO|access-date=18 December 2017|archive-date=22 December 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171222052817/http://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5660/|url-status=live}}</ref> The key site is well-protected and its location, today between a refinery and a sensitive military area, is restricted from public access.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=48}}</ref>

A characteristic element of the Umm Al Nar culture is circular tombs, typically with well-fitted ashlar stones in the outer wall and multiple human remains within.<ref>[http://www.rakmuseum.gov.ae/archaeo.html#uan The Archaeology of Ras al-Khaimah<!-- Bot generated title -->] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090307022811/http://www.rakmuseum.gov.ae/archaeo.html |date=2009-03-07 }}</ref> The Umm Al Nar culture demonstrates evidence of continued trade links with the [[Sumer]]ian and [[Akkadian Empire|Akkadian]] kingdoms and the [[Indus Valley Civilisation|Indus Valley]]. The increasing sophistication of the Umm Al Nar people is demonstrated by a richer material culture and evidence of the domestication and husbandry of animals.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Uerpmann|first=Margarethe|date=2001|title=Remarks on the animal economy of Tell Abraq (Emirates of Sharjah and Umm al-Qaywayn, UAE)|journal=Proceedings of the Seminar for Arabian Studies|volume=31|pages=227–233|jstor=41223685}}</ref><ref name=":13">{{Cite book |author=Magee, Peter |title=The archaeology of prehistoric Arabia : adaptation and social formation from the neolithic to the iron age |date=2014-05-19 |isbn=9780521862318 |location=New York |oclc=852824778}}</ref>

Umm Al Nar was followed by a long 200-year transition to the [[Wadi Suq culture]],<ref name=":04">{{Cite news |title=Societal changes in Bronze Age Arabia... here's the tooth in the matter |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/societal-changes-in-bronze-age-arabia-here-s-the-tooth-in-the-matter-1.466296 |access-date=2017-12-05 |work=The National |language=en}}</ref> which dominated the area from 2000 to 1300 BCE. Key archaeological sites, pointing to major trading cities extant during both periods, are located on the western and eastern coasts of the UAE and in Oman and include Dalma, Umm Al Nar, [[Al Sufouh Archaeological Site|Sufouh]], [[Ed Dur]], [[Tell Abraq]] and [[Kalba]]. Burial sites at [[Shimal]] and [[Seih Al Harf]] in Ras Al Khaimah have evidence of transitional Umm Al Nar to Wadi Suq burials.<ref name="McNabb-2025i">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=195}}</ref>

Camels and other animals were domesticated during the Wadi Suq era (2000-1300 BCE),<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Almathen|first1=Faisal|last2=Charruau|first2=Pauline|last3=Mohandesan|first3=Elmira|last4=Mwacharo|first4=Joram M.|last5=Orozco-terWengel|first5=Pablo|last6=Pitt|first6=Daniel|last7=Abdussamad|first7=Abdussamad M.|last8=Uerpmann|first8=Margarethe|last9=Uerpmann|first9=Hans-Peter|date=2016-06-14|title=Ancient and modern DNA reveal dynamics of domestication and cross-continental dispersal of the dromedary|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|language=en|volume=113|issue=24|pages=6707–6712|doi=10.1073/pnas.1519508113|issn=0027-8424|pmc=4914195|pmid=27162355|bibcode=2016PNAS..113.6707A |doi-access=free}}</ref> leading to increased inland settlement and the cultivation of diverse crops which included the date palm.<ref name=":03">{{Cite news |title=Societal changes in Bronze Age Arabia... here's the tooth in the matter |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/societal-changes-in-bronze-age-arabia-here-s-the-tooth-in-the-matter-1.466296 |access-date=2017-12-05 |work=The National |language=en}}</ref> Increasingly sophisticated metallurgy, pottery and stone carving led to the development of more effective weaponry and other implements, even as trading links with the Indus Valley and [[Mesopotamia]] dwindled and the Wadi Suq people focused increasingly inwards.<ref name=":72">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=58,59}}</ref>

==Iron Age==
{{Main|Iron Age in the United Arab Emirates}}

Throughout three Iron Ages (Iron Age I, 1200–1000 BCE; Iron Age II, 1000–600 BCE; and Iron Age III, 600–300 BCE) and the Hellenistic Mleiha period (after 300 BCE), Southeastern Arabia was occupied by a number of forces (including the [[Achaemenid Empire]]), leading to the construction of a number of fortified settlements.<ref name=":5" />

{{POV|date=March 2026}} The development of increasingly complex irrigation ditches and waterways, ''[[Qanat|falaj]]'' (plural ''aflaj'') took place during the Iron Age II period and finds at Bidaa bint Saud and Thuqeibah<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Mouton, Benoist, Cordoba |date=June 2011 |title=The Snake Figuration in Iron Age Society |url=http://www.na.ae/en/Images/LIWA05.pdf |url-status=dead |journal=Liwa |pages=16–18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180807125234/http://www.na.ae/en/Images/LIWA05.pdf |archive-date=2018-08-07 |access-date=2018-11-27}}</ref> from this period pre-date finds of ''[[qanat]]'' waterways in [[Iran]].<ref name=":05">{{Cite journal |last=Cerro |first=Carmen |date=2013 |title=Biological Remains at Al Madam (Sharjah, UAE) |url=http://www.anthropology.uw.edu.pl/07/bne-07-02.pdf |journal=Bioarchaeology of the Near East |pages=23, 24}}</ref> Early finds of ''aflaj'', particularly those around the desert city of [[Al Ain]], have been cited as the earliest evidence of the construction of these waterways.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=TIKRITI |first=WALID YASIN AL |date=2002 |title=The south-east Arabian origin of the falaj system |journal=Proceedings of the Seminar for Arabian Studies |volume=32 |pages=117–138 |jstor=41223728}}</ref> More and more complex systems of irrigation evolved and the Iron Age II era saw the emergence of centralised authority imposed over access to water<ref name=":76">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=60–63}}</ref> with finds of bronze figures and distinctive pottery testifing to a widespread [[Snake worship|snake cult]], often linked to evidence of collective worship and cohesive communities.<ref name=":5" />

The UAE has an unusual richness of Iron Age archaeological finds, particularly evidenced by the metallurgical centre of [[Saruq Al Hadid]], in the desert to the south of present-day Dubai,<ref>{{Cite news |title=Brushing off sands of time at the archaeological site of Saruq Al-Hadid |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/brushing-off-sands-of-time-at-the-archaeological-site-of-saruq-al-hadid-1.150378 |access-date=2018-09-06 |work=The National}}</ref> which has yielded a trove of thousands of arrow-heads, gold, carnelian and bone jewellery and ceramics - in all over 12,000 items have been recovered from this site alone.<ref name=":5" /> Other important Iron Age settlements in the UAE include [[Al Thuqeibah]], [[Rumailah, UAE|Rumailah]], [[Bidaa Bint Saud|Bidaa bint Saud]], [[Ed Dur]], [[Al Qusais|Qusais]], [[Muweilah]] and [[Tell Abraq]].<ref name="newPerspective">{{Cite book|title=United Arab Emirates: A New Perspective|url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine|url-access=limited|date=2001|publisher=Trident Press|others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter.|isbn=1900724472|location=London|pages=[https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine/page/n48 49]|oclc=47140175}}</ref> 

The Iron Age buildings found at Rumailah are typical of those in the region, at Iron Age I and II sites such as Al Thuqeibah and Muweilah, with a number of row dwellings, although lacking the perimeter walls found at Thuqeibah.<ref name="Potts_al2003">{{Cite book |last1=Potts |first1=Daniel T. |url=https://archive.org/details/archaeologyunite00pott |title=Archaeology of the United Arab Emirates |last2=Nābūdah |first2=Ḥasan Muḥammad |last3=Hellyer |first3=Peter |publisher=[[Trident Press]] |year=2003 |isbn=1-9007-2488-X |location=London |pages=[https://archive.org/details/archaeologyunite00pott/page/n173 174]–177 |oclc=54405078 |url-access=limited}}</ref> A columned hall at Rumailah provides a further link to Muweilah, while a number of pyramidal seals found there echo with similar objects discovered at Bidaa bint Saud.<ref name=":07">{{Cite book |title=Archaeology of the United Arab Emirates |others=Potts, Daniel T.; Nābūdah, Ḥasan Muḥammad; Hellyer, Peter |year=2003 |isbn=190072488X |location=London |pages=188 |oclc=54405078}}</ref>

Enabled by the [[domestication of the camel]] in the region, thought to have taken place around 1000 BCE, Muweilah's trade included the manufacture of copper goods, with "extensive casting spillage from the manufacture of copper items found throughout the site".<ref name=":032">{{Cite web |last=UAEINTERACT |title=UAE Interact, United Arab Emirates information, news, photographs, maps and webcams |url=http://www.uaeinteract.com/history/e_walk/con_4/con4_47.asp |access-date=2017-12-04 |website=www.uaeinteract.com}}</ref> Muweilah is relatively unique in its early and extensive adoption of iron goods, thought to have been imported from Iran.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Abed |first1=Ibrahim |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QcMz3zV0qAMC&q=before+the+emirates&pg=PA28 |title=United Arab Emirates: A New Perspective |last2=Hellyer |first2=Peter |date=2001 |publisher=Trident Press Ltd |isbn=9781900724470 |language=en}}</ref> Hundreds of grinding stones indicate the consumption of both barley and wheat. Artefacts recovered from Muweilah are contiguous to those found from the same period at Tell Abraq and other Iron Age settlements, evidence of an emerging uniform material across the settled areas of the time.<ref name=":76" />

The Iron Age II period also saw the construction of fortifications, with a number of towers and other buildings offering protection to ''aflaj'' and the crops they watered.<ref name=":7">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=64}}</ref> Hili 14 in Al Ain, Madhab Fort and [[Awhala Fort]] in [[Fujairah]] as well as [[Jebel Buhais]] near [[Al Madam|Madam]] in [[Sharjah]] and [[Rafaq]] and [[Al Nasla]] in the [[Wadi Qor]] in [[Ras Al Khaimah]] are all fortifications dating to this time.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine |title=United Arab Emirates : a new perspective |date=2001 |publisher=Trident Press |others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter. |isbn=1900724472 |location=London |pages=[https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine/page/n48 49] |oclc=47140175 |url-access=limited}}</ref>

== Mleiha ==
[[File:Mleiha bronze roman era statue of a griffin third century BC.jpg|thumb|alt=Small statue of a winged creature|Roman statue of a [[griffin]] found in Mleiha]]
The ancient Near Eastern city of Mleiha, today inland of Sharjah, is linked to the port city of Ed Dur on the west coast of the UAE at Umm Al Quwain.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Faisal Masudi |date=2015-10-28 |title=Two ancient UAE towns linked global trade |url=http://gulfnews.com/news/uae/society/two-ancient-uae-towns-linked-global-trade-1.1609036 |access-date=2017-10-26 |work=[[Gulf News]]}}</ref> It emerged as an important settlement in the late Iron Age and continued to be a major area of settlement into the pre-Islamic era, and offers the most complete evidence of human settlement and material production in the area at that time.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine |title=United Arab Emirates: a new perspective |date=2001 |publisher=Trident Press |others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter. |isbn=978-1900724470 |location=London |pages=[https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine/page/n52 53] |oclc=47140175 |url-access=limited}}</ref> The period from 300 to 0 BCE has been variously called the 'Mleiha period', the 'Late Pre-Islamic period' (PIR) and, according to older references, the 'Hellenistic era': however, [[Wars of Alexander the Great|Alexander the Great's]] conquests went no further than Persia, and left Arabia untouched.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine |title=United Arab Emirates: a new perspective |date=2001 |publisher=Trident Press |others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter. |isbn=978-1900724470 |location=London |pages=[https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine/page/n50 51] |oclc=47140175 |url-access=limited}}</ref>

Although not conquered by Alexander, Mleiha certainly reflected Greek cultural influence and troves of Hellenistic coins were found at both at Ed Dur and Mleiha featuring a head of Heracles and a seated Zeus on the obverse, and bearing the name of ''Abi'el'' in Aramaic.<ref>{{Cite news |title=500 tombs dating back 2,000 years found in Umm Al Quwain |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/500-tombs-dating-back-2-000-years-found-in-umm-al-quwain-1.106172 |access-date=2017-10-26 |work=The National |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=15 July 2021 |title=Treasure trove of priceless silver coins discovered in Sharjah |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/2021/07/15/treasure-trove-of-priceless-silver-coins-discovered-in-sharjah/ |access-date=2021-07-15 |website=The National}}</ref> These [[Tetradrachm|tetradrachms]] match coin moulds found at Mleiha, which (with finds of Mleiha slag)<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 April 2016 |title=Sharjah's Mleiha Archaeological Centre - a step back in time |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/arts-culture/sharjah-s-mleiha-archaeological-centre-a-step-back-in-time-1.137537 |access-date=2018-12-06 |website=The National |language=en}}</ref> suggest the existence of a metallurgical centre<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine |title=United Arab Emirates: a new perspective |date=2001 |publisher=Trident Press |others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter. |isbn=978-1900724470 |location=London |pages=[https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine/page/n57 58] |oclc=47140175 |url-access=limited}}</ref> and indicate a link between Mleiha and Ed Dur. Camels and horses buried with their heads reversed are also a common feature of animal burials at both Ed Dur and Mleiha.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Van Neer |first1=Wim |last2=Gautier |first2=Achilles |last3=Haerinck |first3=Ernie |last4=Wouters |first4=Wim |last5=Kaptijn |first5=Eva |date=2017-05-01 |title=Animal exploitation at ed-Dur (Umm al-Qaiwain, United Arab Emirates) |journal=Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy |language=en |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=11–30 |doi=10.1111/aae.12080 |issn=1600-0471 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

Funerary inscriptions dating from the mid-3rd century BCE and found at Mleiha mention a kingdom of "Uman": the kingdom is also mentioned by Greek writers [[Pliny the Elder]] and [[Strabo]] as ''Omana''. The Mleiha site was apparently abandoned after the 3rd century CE, marking the kingdom's fall.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Centre |first=UNESCO World Heritage |title=Mleiha, Late Pre-Islamic Center of a South-East Arabian Kingdom |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/6643/ |access-date=2023-04-14 |website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre |language=en}}</ref>

== <span class="anchor" id="Early Christian presence in the United Arab Emirates"></span>Early Christian presence ==
Archaeology has revealed [[Late antiquity|late-antique]] and early Islamic era Christian communities in the present-day UAE, linked to the [[Church of the East]] in the  [[Christianity in Eastern Arabia#Beth Qatraye|Beth Qaṭraye]] region along the coast.<ref>Robert A. Carter, "Christianity in the Gulf during the first centuries of Islam," ''Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy'', vol. 19, no. 1 (2008), pp. 71–108, https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1600-0471.2008.00293.x.</ref> On Sir Bani Yas (Abu Dhabi), a monastery and church were first identified in 1992; subsequent study established the site's monastic character, and a 2025 excavation uncovered a moulded-plaster cross in adjacent courtyard houses (indicating a broader monastic settlement).<ref>G. R. D. King, "A Nestorian monastic settlement on the island of Ṣīr Banī Yās, Abu Dhabi: a preliminary report," ''Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies'', vol. 60, no. 2 (1997), pp. 221–235, https://doi.org/10.1017/S0041977X00036375.</ref><ref>Abu Dhabi Media Office, "Department of Culture and Tourism – Abu Dhabi discovers ancient cross in latest archaeological excavation at monastery site on Sir Bani Yas Island," press release, 19 August 2025, https://www.mediaoffice.abudhabi/en/tourism/department-of-culture-and-tourism-abu-dhabi-discovers-ancient-cross-in-latest-archaeological-excavation-at-monastery-site-on-sir-bani-yas-island/.</ref><ref>Kristina Killgrove, "'We never had concrete proof': Archaeologists discover Christian cross in Abu Dhabi, proving 1,400-year-old site was a monastery," ''Live Science'', 22 August 2025, https://www.livescience.com/archaeology/we-never-had-concrete-proof-archaeologists-discover-christian-cross-in-abu-dhabi-proving-1-400-year-old-site-was-a-monastery.</ref> A second monastery was identified on [[Siniyah Island]] in Umm Al Quwain with radiocarbon dates from 534 to 656 CE, representing part of a cluster of Gulf Christian sites contemporary with the emergence of Islam.<ref>Timothy Power, Michele Degli Esposti, Robert Hoyland & Rania Kannouma, "A newly discovered Late Antique monastery and Islamic town on Sīnīya Island, Umm al-Quwain," ''Proceedings of the Seminar for Arabian Studies'', vol. 52 (2023), pp. 273–291, https://archaeopresspublishing.com/ojs/index.php/PSAS/article/view/1644.</ref><ref>Associated Press, "Christian Monastery Possibly Predating Islam Found in UAE," ''Voice of America'', 4 November 2022, https://www.voanews.com/a/christian-monastery-possibly-predating-islam-found-in-uae/6819647.html.</ref>

The [[Tuwwam]] region has long been held to have been related to the [[Buraimi]] settlement as a result of mentions in literature by [[Al-Maqdisi|Muqaddasi]] and others. However, contemporary research has tentatively identified Siniyah Island in Umm Al Quwain as the centre of the ancient lost town and region of Tawwam (or Tu'am); the name ''Tu'am'' is derived from [[Thomas the Apostle|St Thomas the Apostle of the East]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Power |first=Tim |date=2024-06-21 |title=How the excavation of Tu'am will help fill in the holes of our historical knowledge |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/opinion/comment/2024/06/21/a-curiously-named-lost-city-has-deep-significance-for-the-uae/ |access-date=2024-06-22 |website=The National |language=en}}</ref> Siniyah is the site of both a major Christian monastic complex and the oldest [[Pearl hunting|pearling]] town in the Gulf.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-03-20 |title=Their world was the oyster: Oldest pearl town found in UAE |url=https://apnews.com/article/archaeology-uae-oldest-pearling-town-e22516065e1a596eabaa5dad27e2b28f |access-date=2023-03-20 |website=AP NEWS |language=en}}</ref> Siniyah and Sir Bani Yas are two of six monasteries identified on the Persian Gulf coast.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dennehy |first=John |date=2022-11-03 |title=Ancient Christian monastery discovered in Umm Al Quwain |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/heritage/2022/11/03/ancient-christian-monastery-discovered-in-umm-al-quwain/ |access-date=2023-03-20 |website=The National |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Beech |first1=Dr Mark Jonathan |date=1 January 2019 |title=The Church and Monastery of Sir Bani Yas — An Archaeological Site of the 7th and 8th centuries CE |url=https://www.academia.edu/44912348 |access-date=21 February 2022 |website=Department of Culture and Tourism (DCT Abu Dhabi) — Site Guide Booklet (38 pages)}}</ref>

==Islam and the Middle Ages==
{{main|Islam in the United Arab Emirates}}

{{See also|Battle of Dibba}}
The arrival of envoys from [[Muhammad]] in 632 heralded the region's conversion to [[Islam]]. At this time, Sasanian influence over the area of Southeastern Arabia was projected by a number of fortified outposts. The Sasanian governor was based on the Batinah coast in present-day Oman, at Damsetjerd. He rejected the message from Medinah, while the local Al Azd tribes under their Julanda leaders accepted it. An uprising against the Sasanians at Damsetjerd followed and they were ejected from the area with the loss of their arms and wealth.<ref name=":15">{{Cite book|title=United Arab Emirates : a new perspective|url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine|url-access=limited|date=2001|publisher=Trident Press|others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter.|isbn=1900724472|location=London|pages=81|oclc=47140175}}</ref>

Following the death of Muhammad, many of the tribes of the Gulf turned their backs on the religion of Islam, a wave of apostasy that led the first Caliph, Abu Bakr to launch a series of campaigns to suppress the rebellions. The final battle of what was to become known as the Ridda Wars was fought at Dibba on the east coast of the present day Emirates against a local uprising of apostate forces led by Laqit bin Malik Al Azdi, resulting in the triumph of Islam in the Arabian Peninsula.<ref name="Morton2016" /> Although Arab historians record the deaths of 10,000 men in the Battle of Dibba, a figure which has passed into local legend, this was a figure of speech, often used to signify great loss of life, rather than an accurate toll of death.<ref name=":15" />

The Islamic port of Julfar, the precursor settlement to Ras Al Khaimah in the present day Emirates, is prominent in the earliest mentions of Islamic era activity in the region when a force of 3,000 men was sent by the Caliph Abu Bakr from Julfar to invade the Sasanian island of Qays and then proceed to take Fars and move into the Iranian interior.<ref name=":16">{{Cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/unitedarabemirat00vine |title=United Arab Emirates : a new perspective |date=2001 |publisher=Trident Press |others=Abed, Ibrahim., Hellyer, Peter. |isbn=1900724472 |location=London |pages=85-87 |oclc=47140175 |url-access=limited}}</ref>

A period of instability followed that saw the [[List of Abbasid caliphs|Abbasid caliphs]] (who took power in 750) attempt to assert their authority over Southeastern Arabia and undertake invasions of Julfar and the surrounding areas. Repelled by the local tribes on a number of occasions, all of Southeastern Arabia from Julfar to [[Nizwa]] in present day Oman fell to the 25,000 strong army of Muhammad Bin Nur following the accession of the Caliph Al Murtadid in 892 CE.<ref name=":16" /> The widespread destruction of this force brought the entire region down and it was to be some two hundred years before the waning influence of the Caliphate was to see local leaders establish themselves as influences in the region, with the emergence of wealthy trading ports at Hulayla and Julfar in Ras Al Khaimah, Sohar in Oman and the island of Hormuz.<ref name=":16" />

The Islamic era saw the expansion of trading links to the East, with trade centred around [[Hormuz Island]] and its port of [[Julfar]]. The medieval port was settled in two halves in the present-day Ras Al Khaimah suburbs of [[Al Mataf]] and Al Nudud from the 13th to 17th centuries CE. Julfar's founding has been dated to the early to mid-14th century as a small settlement of palm-frond huts which expanded during the 15th and 16th centuries into an important trading town.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Carter |first1=Robert Andrew |last2=Zhao |first2=Bing |last3=Lane |first3=Kevin |last4=Velde |first4=Christian |date=2020 |title=The rise and ruin of a medieval port town: A reconsideration of the development of Julfar |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/aae.12162 |journal=Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy |language=en |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=501–523 |doi=10.1111/aae.12162 |issn=0905-7196}}</ref> Julfar's early population fished and probably pearled; they also farmed, benefiting from the access to land and sea which characterised Kush, Julfar's predecessor settlement.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kennet |first=Derek |date=1997 |title=Kush: A Sasanian and Islamic-period archaeological tell in Ras Al Khaimah |journal=Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy |language=en |doi=10.1111/(issn)1600-0471}}</ref> Evidence of occupation at Julfar include finds of 14th-century Chinese [[porcelain]], followed by post holes and ovens and the development of mud-brick buildings, defined streets and courtyard houses as the town developed into the 15th century.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kennet |first=Derek |date=2003 |title=Julfar and the urbanisation of Southeast Arabia |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1034/j.1600-0471.2003.00011.x |journal=Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy |language=en |volume=14 |issue=1 |pages=103–125 |doi=10.1034/j.1600-0471.2003.00011.x |issn=0905-7196 |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Julfar's development as a major trading centre has been linked to the emergence of [[Kingdom of Hormuz|Hormuz]] in the 14th and 15th centuries.<ref name=":52">{{Cite book |last=Power |first=Timothy |url=https://www.worldcat.org/title/987637211 |title=The ports of Oman |date=2017 |publisher=Georg Olms Verlag |isbn=978-3-487-15391-9 |editor-last=Al-Salimi |editor-first=Abdulrahman |series=Studies on Ibadism and Oman |location=Hildesheim |pages=219–230 |oclc=987637211 |editor-last2=Staples |editor-first2=Eric}}</ref> The 'Hormuzi boom' of the time followed the occupation of the island of Jarun in the [[Strait of Hormuz]] by Mahmud Qalhati and his people and its subsequent emergence as a global trading hub linking the extensive Eastern Arab trade networks<ref name=":22">{{Cite journal |last=Kennet |first=Derek |date=1 Jan 2002 |title=The development of Northern Ras al-Khaimah and the 14th-century Hormuzi economic boom in the lower Gulf |journal=Proceedings of the Seminar for Arabian Studies |volume=32 |pages=151–164}}</ref> Hormuz was entirely without sweet water and largely without food production resources and it is likely that Julfar provided pearls, food and water to meet demand on the island, which led to the extensive agricultural development of the areas inland of Julfar.<ref name=":14">{{Cite journal |last=Kennet |first=Derek |date=2003 |title=Julfar and the urbanisation of Southeast Arabia |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1034/j.1600-0471.2003.00011.x |journal=Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy |language=en |volume=14 |issue=1 |pages=103–125 |doi=10.1034/j.1600-0471.2003.00011.x |issn=0905-7196 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> Julfar as a centre for the export of pearls would also be linked to its role as a regional centre of trade between the mainland and Hormuz.<ref name=":14" /> A tributary of the Kingdom of Hormuz, which also exercised authority over the areas comprising modern day Bahrain, Qatar and Oman, direct Hormuzi control over Julfar was exercised.<ref name=":32">{{Cite journal |last1=Carter |first1=Robert Andrew |last2=Zhao |first2=Bing |last3=Lane |first3=Kevin |last4=Velde |first4=Christian |date=2020 |title=The rise and ruin of a medieval port town: A reconsideration of the development of Julfar |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/aae.12162 |journal=Arabian Archaeology and Epigraphy |language=en |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=501–523 |doi=10.1111/aae.12162 |issn=0905-7196}}</ref>

Julfar is mentioned by Portuguese explorer [[Duarte Barbosa]] as a major entrepôt, and Barbosa (writing in 1518) also mentions Julfar's successor settlement, Ras Al Khaimah – evidence of a period of contiguity: "Passing above this place ''Profam'' [Khor Fakkan], we come to another called ''Julfar'', where dwell persons of worth, great navigators and wholesale dealers. Here is a very great fishery as well of seed-pearls as of large pearls, and the Moors of Ormus come hither to buy them and carry them to India and many other lands. The trade of this place brings in a great revenue to the King of Ormus … Beyond these ''Profam'' villages are others along the coast, one of which is a large place called ''Reçoyma'' [Ras al-Khaimah]."<ref name=":6">{{Cite book |last=Barbosa |first=Duarte |title=The Book of Duarte Barbosa |publisher=Haklyut Society |year=1918 |location=London |pages=73}}</ref>
[[File:Atlas-de-lazaro-luis_Cidade_de_Qatar.jpg|right|thumb|''Sidade de Julfar'' in Lázaro Luís' 1563 map of Arabia]]
Other travellers mentioning Julfar at this time included the Portuguese explorer [[Pedro Álvares Cabral]] and Italian traveller [[Ludovico di Varthema]].<ref name=":6" />

Julfar's prominent role in the region's pearl trade was by now so great that Portuguese conquistador [[Pedro Teixeira|Pedro Texeira]] postulated the origin of the Portuguese word for pearl, ''aljofar'', originated with the name Al Julfar.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Teixeira |first=Pedro |title=The Travels of Pedro Teixeira |date=1902 |publisher=Hakluyt Society |pages=217}}</ref>

=== Pearling ===
Pearls fished from the Persian Gulf were prized in antiquity: known to the Sumerians as 'fish eyes', they famously adorned Cleopatra and by the 16th century were driving an industry that employed two to three thousands boats in the seasonal fisheries and brought in an income estimated at the time at over half a million [[Portuguese cruzado]]s.<ref name="Fer">{{cite journal |last1=Fernandes |first1=Agnelo Paulo |date=June 2009 |title=The Portuguese Cartazes System and the 'Magumbayas' on Pearl Fishing in the Gulf |url=https://www.na.ae/en/pdfviewer.aspx?path=%2Fen%2FImages%2FLIWA01.pdf |url-status=live |journal=Liwa |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=12–14 |issn=1729-9039 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220517070810/https://www.na.ae/en/pdfviewer.aspx?path=%2Fen%2FImages%2FLIWA01.pdf |archive-date=17 May 2022 |access-date=11 April 2019}}</ref> Workers in Julfar and surrounding areas were paid in pearls instead of cash.<ref name="Fer" /> 

After the [[Portuguese Empire]] occupied Hormuz island and the northern and Persian coasts and took over the taxation system, the Sultan of Hormuz was responsible for collecting pearling taxes (''magumbayas'') from 1523 to 1622 and Portugal obtained revenue from the industry until the second decade of the 18th century. The Portuguese system issued navigation permits (''cartazes''); permit distribution and tax collection were centered in the ports extending from [[Julfar]] to [[Khasab]]. On the Persian coast, the Portuguese did the same with the Arabs from [[Kong, Iran|Bandar Kong]] to the southwest coast of Iran. Any ship sailing without a Portuguese license was subject to capture by the [[Portuguese India Armadas|Portuguese Armada]]. Fear of armada cannons led to maritime control of the Arabian coast.<ref name="Fer" />

== Portuguese empire: 16th–18th centuries ==
[[File:WELLSTED(1838) p1.027 MAP OF OMAN (retouched).jpg|thumb|upright=1.4|alt=See caption|1838 Map of Oman, showing the peninsula that became the United Arab Emirates in 1971]]
[[File:Persian Gulf z1507-1750.gif|thumb|alt=Map of the Persian Gulf region|Portuguese colonies in Arabia]]
[[File:Forte_de_doba.jpg|thumb|alt=A simple 17th-century map|[[:pt:Forte de Doba|Doba Fort]], built by the [[Portuguese Empire]] in [[Dibba Al-Hisn]] in 1620.<ref name=":2" />]]
[[File:Corfacão.jpg|alt=Painting of a fort and surroundings, seen from above|thumb|1635 painting of the Portuguese fortress Khor Fakkan (Corfacão)<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Livro das plantas de todas as fortalezas, cidades e povoaçoens do Estado da India Oriental / António Bocarro [1635].|url=http://purl.pt/27184/4/1891517_PDF/1891517_PDF_24-C-R0150/1891517_0000_capa-capa_t24-C-R0150.pdf|access-date=2021-11-01|archive-date=2021-11-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211104205412/https://purl.pt/27184/4/1891517_PDF/1891517_PDF_24-C-R0150/1891517_0000_capa-capa_t24-C-R0150.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref>]]

The [[Kingdom of Portugal]] was refounded following the ''[[Reconquista]]'', the re-establishment of Christian over Islamic rule in [[Spain]] and Portugual, and almost immediately began a series of expansionist moves against Arab ports in North and West Africa.<ref name=":722" /> Rounding the [[Cape of Good Hope]], the Portuguese expansion across West Africa was to culminate in [[Vasco da Gama|Vasco Da Gama]]’s recruitment of a [[Gujarati people|Gujarati]] pilot in [[Malindi]], in present day [[Kenya]], and the discovery of the route to [[India]].<ref name=":722">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=110}}</ref>

Da Gama’s explorations of India demonstrated to the Portuguese the enormous wealth of the Arab Eastern trade network and Da Gama’s successor under [[Manuel I of Portugal|King Manuel]], [[Afonso de Albuquerque]], embarked on a mission to establish choke points at [[Socotra]] and Hormuz in order to throttle the Arab trade and then take over the key trading points on that network, including [[Aden]], Hormuz, [[Goa]] and [[Malacca]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Loureiro |first=RM |date=2015 |title=The Portuguese in Hormuz and the Trade in Chinese Porcelain |journal=Bulletin of Portuguese Japanese Studies |volume=5 |issue=26 |pages=5-26}}</ref>

de Albuquerque embarked on his expedition against the Arabs in 1506, establishing a fort at Socotra before proceeding to invade Muscat, then a [[Kingdom of Hormuz|Hormuzi]] dependency. Having sacked Muscat and pulled down the minarets of its mosques, he proceeded against the Omani port of [[Sohar]], which capitulated, and then to Khor Fakkan, in the present day Emirates.<ref name=":8" /> At the time Khor Fakkan, also a Hormuzi port, was an important port of disembarkation to India.<ref name=":723" /> Local resistance was no match for trained Portuguese soldiers with [[Arquebus|arqubusiers]] and other advanced weaponry and Khor Fakkan fell. de Albuquerque, as was often the case with the Portuguese in conquest, had the ears and noses of all the men of the town cut off.<ref name=":8">{{Cite book |last=Albuquerque |first=AD |title=The Commentaries of the Great Alfonso Dalboquerque |date=1744 |publisher=Haklyut Society |year=1744 |location=London |publication-date=1744}}</ref>

de Albuquerque next sailed to Hormuz, where he found a force had been assembled against him of some 60 large vessels and 200 smaller boats and many more shore-boats together with a land force of some 15-20,000 men. Unprepared for the sustained fire from the three Portuguese warships, the Arab fleet was immediately thrown into disarray and completely overwhelmed with great loss of life.<ref name=":723" /> On 10 October 1507, the Portuguese conquest of Hormuz was complete and construction was started on the Portuguese fort of ‘Our Lady of the Victory’, which used the anchor stone of the largest of the Arab ships, the ''Meri'', as the lintel of its front door.<ref name=":723">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=98-123}}</ref>

The Portuguese found their new conquests difficult to maintain against the emergence not only of local forces but of new enemies. Despite moving against Julfar in 1621, they found themselves routed from Hormuz in 1623 by a joint force of Persians under [[Abbas the Great|Shah Abbas]] supported by a new force in the region: the English. Muscat was retaken by a rebellion under the [[Ya'rubids|Yaaruba]] in 1650<ref>{{Cite web |title=Portuguese era |url=https://www.nla.ae/en/our-history/historical-periods/portuguese-era/ |access-date=2026-04-08}}</ref> and, although the Portuguese built and garrisoned forts around Southeastern Arabia, at Julfar, [[Dibba]], [[Al Bidya|Bidya]], Khor Fakkan, [[Kalba]] and along the [[Al Batinah Region|Batinah]] Coast of Oman, these were all to fall to local forces.<ref name=":723" />

Throughout the following century, foreign and local forces ebbed and flowed in the region, as the Portuguese, [[Dutch East India Company|Dutch]], [[East India Company|English]], [[France|French]], Persians and Arabs vied for influence and supremacy. The East India companies of the expanding European powers were to become the dominant commercial and military forces not only in the Gulf but across the former Arab trading networks to the East.<ref name=":724">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=130-135}}</ref> Portuguese influence in the area waned as British influence grew.<ref name=":724" />

== <span class="anchor" id="British empire: 19th–20th century"></span>British empire: 19th and 20th centuries ==
{{See also|Trucial States|Piracy in the Persian Gulf}}

The [[Huwala people|Huwala]], the Arab settlers of the areas formerly under Hormuzi governance on the southern coast of Iran and the islands in the Gulf, were now to re-emerge under the influence of a new force in the region: the [[Al Qasimi|Qawasim]] (singular Al Qasimi). A powerful maritime federation of Arab tribes and trading centres, the Qawasim were inevitably to come into conflict with the encroaching forces of the British East India Company.<ref name=":724" />

From the early 18th century, the Al Qawasim of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah emerged as a maritime power under [[Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi|Rahmah bin Mattar Al Qasimi]], controlling ports and islands on both sides of the Gulf and building regional trading networks.<ref name=":725">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=144-166}}</ref> Later British narratives, particularly those of historian [[John Gordon Lorimer (civil servant)|John Gordon Lorimer]], portrayed them as pirates, but this interpretation has been challenged, most notably by [[Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi|Sheikh Dr Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi]], who argues that accusations of piracy often masked imperial rivalry and strategic ambition.<ref>{{Cite book |last=al-Qāsimī |first=Sulṭān ibn Muḥammad |title=The myth of Arab piracy in the Gulf |date=1986 |publisher=Croom Helm |isbn=0709921063 |location=London |oclc=12583612}}</ref>

Early clashes with the British East India Company — beginning in 1727 when the British attacked the rival trading post established by the Qawasim at [[Qeshm Island|Qeshm]] — were rooted in commercial competition rather than piracy.<ref name=":725" /> Throughout the mid-to-late 1700s, the Qawasim were deeply entangled in shifting alliances and conflicts involving Persia, Oman and other Gulf powers. Far from acting as indiscriminate pirates, they frequently engaged in conventional warfare, negotiated truces and participated in regional power struggles.<ref name=":725" /> By the turn of the 19th century, rising [[Wahhabism|Wahhabi]] influence, Omani succession struggles and expanding British naval involvement created a volatile environment of ongoing conflict in which the British, allied to the [[List of rulers of Oman|Sultan of Muscat]] with whom the Qawasim were at war, became embroiled. A series of incidents involving British vessels such as the ''Bassein'', ''Viper'', ''Shannon'' and ''Trimmer'' occurred largely within the context of the local conflict.<ref name=":725" />

Internal tensions grew within the Qawasim leadership as Wahhabi hardliners gained influence, eventually undermining the Qawasim Ruler [[Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi]], who had previously sought accommodation with the British. With Wahhabi-backed forces encouraging aggressive maritime campaigns and British shipping threatened, the East India Company resolved to act decisively. By 1809, amid escalating hostilities and competing alliances, the stage was set for a major British expedition against the Qawasim.<ref name=":725" />

=== Persian Gulf campaign of 1809 ===
{{main|Persian Gulf campaign of 1809}}

[[File:Ras Al Khaimah under attack, 1809 01.jpg|thumb|upright=1.25|alt=See caption|1813 painting of the 1809 sacking of the port of Ras Al Khaimah.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |author=Sulṭān ibn Muḥammad al-Qāsimī |title=The myth of Arab piracy in the Gulf |date=1986 |publisher=Croom Helm |isbn=978-0709921066 |location=London |oclc=12583612}}</ref>]]

An expeditionary force embarked for Ras Al Khaimah in 1809, beginning the Persian Gulf campaign of 1809 following years of tension between the British and Al Qawasim navies. The campaign ended with a peace treaty between the British and [[Hassan bin Rahma Al Qasimi]], the Al Qasimi leader, which broke down in 1815. British diplomat J. G. Lorimer wrote that after the dissolution of the treaty, the Al Qasimi "indulged in a carnival of maritime lawlessness, to which even their own previous record presented no parallel."<ref name="qdl1">{{cite web|url=http://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575944.0x0000c5|title='Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf. Vol I. Historical. Part IA & IB. J G Lorimer. 1915' [653] (796/1782)|publisher=qdl.qa|access-date=13 January 2014|archive-date=26 June 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190626201145/https://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575944.0x0000c5|url-status=live}} ''This article incorporates text from this source, which is in the [[public domain]].''</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575944.0x0000c6|title='Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf. Vol I. Historical. Part IA & IB. J G Lorimer. 1915' [654] (797/1782)|date=30 September 2014|publisher=qdl.qa|access-date=4 August 2015|archive-date=24 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924084344/http://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575944.0x0000c6|url-status=live}} ''This article incorporates text from this source, which is in the [[public domain]].''</ref>

After another year of recurring attacks, Hassan bin Rahmah made conciliatory overtures to Bombay at the end of 1818 which were "sternly rejected". Naval resources commanded by the Al Qasimi were estimated at 60 large boats headquartered in Ras Al Khaimah, carrying 80 to 300 men each, and 40 smaller vessels housed in nearby ports.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575944.0x0000c8|title='Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf. Vol I. Historical. Part IA & IB. J G Lorimer. 1915' [656] (799/1782)|date=30 September 2014|publisher=qdl.qa|access-date=26 September 2018|archive-date=27 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180927005523/https://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575944.0x0000c8|url-status=live}} ''This article incorporates text from this source, which is in the [[public domain]].''</ref>

=== Persian Gulf campaign of 1819 and General Maritime Treaty of 1820 ===
{{Main|Persian Gulf campaign of 1819|General Maritime Treaty of 1820}}

[[File:صورة 003.jpg|thumb|upright=1.25|alt=A painting of soldiers, a fortress, and burning ships|1813 painting of [[Laft]] under attack by British forces in December 1809.<ref name=":3" />]]
{{Wikisource|General Treaty for the Cessation of Plunder and Piracy by Land and Sea}}

The British began an [[Expeditionary warfare|expedition]] against the Qawasim in November 1819, besieging Ras Al Khaimah with a platoon of 3,000 soldiers led by Major General William Keir Grant and supported by a number of warships which included the [[HMS Liverpool (1814)|HMS ''Liverpool'']] and [[HMS Curlew (1812)|''Curlew'']]. They extended an offer to [[Said bin Sultan]] of [[Muscat]] to make him ruler of the Pirate Coast if he agreed to assist the British in their expedition, and he obliged with a force of 600 men and two ships.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575945.0x000003|title='Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf. Vol I. Historical. Part IA & IB. J G Lorimer. 1915' [659] (802/1782)|date=30 September 2014|publisher=qdl.qa|access-date=4 August 2015|archive-date=26 June 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190626201132/https://www.qdl.qa/en/archive/81055/vdc_100023575945.0x000003|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=In Defiance of The Elements: A Personal View of Qatar|last=Moorehead|first=John|publisher=Quartet Books|year=1977|isbn=9780704321496|page=23}}</ref>

With the fall of Ras Al Khaimah and the surrender of [[Dhayah Fort]], the British established a garrison of 800 [[sepoy]]s and artillery in Ras Al Khaimah. [[Al Jazirah Al Hamra|Jazirat Al Hamra]], south of Rad Al Khaimah, was found to be deserted. The British destroyed the fortifications and larger vessels in [[Umm Al Quwain]], [[Ajman]], [[Al Heera|Fasht]], [[Sharjah]], [[Abu Hail]], and Dubai; ten vessels which had taken shelter in Bahrain were also destroyed.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf|last=Lorimer|first=John|publisher=British Government, Bombay|year=1915|pages=669}}</ref> After the expedition, the British and the [[List of rulers of individual Emirates of the United Arab Emirates|sheikh]]s of the coastal communities signed the [[General Maritime Treaty of 1820]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=178}}</ref>[[File:United Arab Emirates. 1870 Johnson Map of Turkey, Persia, Arabia, Balochistan (cropped).jpg|thumb|upright=1.4|alt=A map in several colours|1870 map of the Trucial Coast]]
[[File:Flag of the Trucial States (1968–1971).svg|thumb|alt=Flag with red bars top and bottom and a green seven-pointed star on a white background|Flag of the Trucial States Council]]
[[File:Suwaidi_pearls_02.jpg|thumb|alt=Pearls on a red cloth|Pearls from [[Rams (Ras Al Khaimah)|Rams]]. Pearls were central to the economy of the Trucial States.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=United Arab Emirates University |last2=Aqil |first2=K. |date=2018 |title=PEARL INDUSTRY IN THE UAE REGION IN 1869-1938: ITS CONSTRUCTION, REPRODUCTION, AND DECLINE |url=http://journals.rudn.ru/sociology/article/view/19182 |journal=RUDN Journal of Sociology |volume=18 |issue=3 |pages=452–469 |doi=10.22363/2313-2272-2018-18-3-452-469|doi-access=free }}</ref>]]The 1820 treaty was followed by the 1847 "Engagement to Prohibit Exportation of Slaves From Africa on board of Vessels Belonging to Bahrain and to the Trucial States" and the "Allow Right of Search of April–May 1847".<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=288|oclc=64689681}}</ref> The signatory sheikhs, now expanded by conquering smaller neighbours, included [[Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi|Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr]] of Ras Al Khaimah, [[Hasher bin Maktoum|Sheikh Hasher bin Maktoum]] of Dubai, [[Abdelaziz bin Rashid Al Nuaimi|Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Rashid]] of Ajman, [[Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla|Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid]] of Umm Al Quwain and [[Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan|Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnoun]] of Abu Dhabi.<ref name="Lorimer 1915">{{Cite book |last=Lorimer |first=John |title=Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf Volume II |publisher=British Government, Bombay |year=1915 |pages=2480}}</ref>

The treaty, granting protection to British vessels, did not prevent coastal wars between tribes. Intermittent raids continued until 1835, when the sheikhs agreed not to engage in hostilities at sea for one year in a treaty negotiated by British resident [[Samuel Hennell]]; Hennell's truce was renewed annually until 1853.<ref name="McNabb-2025i" />

=== Perpetual Maritime Truce ===
{{Main|Perpetual Maritime Truce}}

The Perpetual Maritime Truce, signed on 4 May 1853, replaced the annual treaties and prohibited any act of aggression at sea. The signatories included Abdulla bin Rashid of Umm Al Quwain, Hamed bin Rashid of Ajman, [[Saeed bin Butti]] of Dubai, [[Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan|Saeed bin Tahnoun]] ("Chief of the Beniyas") and Sultan bin Saqr ("Chief of the Joasmees").<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates : a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=286|oclc=64689681}}</ref> A further agreement suppressing the slave trade was signed in 1856, followed by the "Additional Article to the Maritime Truce Providing for the Protection of the Telegraph Line and Stations, Dated 1864". An agreement about the treatment of absconding debtors followed in June 1879.<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=211|oclc=64689681}}</ref><ref>Perpetual Maritime Truce of 4 May 1853</ref>

=== Exclusive Agreement ===
{{Main|Exclusive Agreement}}

Signed in March 1892, the Exclusive Agreement bound the Trucial Rulers not to enter into "any agreement or correspondence with any Power other than the British Government"; without British assent, they would not "consent to the residence within my territory of the agent of any other government" and would not "cede, sell, mortgage or otherwise give for occupation any part of my territory, save to the British Government".<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=293|oclc=64689681}}</ref> In return, the British promised to protect the Trucial Coast from hostile activity on land and sea.<ref>{{Cite web |title=United Arab Emirates (02/02) |url=https://2009-2017.state.gov/outofdate/bgn/unitedarabemirates/26479.htm |access-date=2024-06-16 |website=U.S. Department of State}}</ref>

=== Trucial States affairs ===
{{Main|Trucial States}}

In accordance with the treaties, the Trucial rulers were independent to manage their internal affairs. They often asked the British to provide naval firepower for their frequent disputes, particularly when the disputes involved indebtedness to British and Indian nationals.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=273}}</ref>

During the late 19th and early 20th centuries, a number of changes occurred to the status of [[emirate]]s. Emirates such as Rams and Dayah (now part of Ras Al Khaimah) were signatories to the original 1819 treaty, but were not recognised by the British as trucial states; the emirate of [[Fujairah]], today one of the seven United Arab Emirates, was not recognised as a trucial state until 1952. Kalba, recognised as a trucial state by the British in 1936, is part of the present-day [[emirate of Sharjah]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|oclc=64689681}}</ref>

Until the 1930s, the British refrained from interfering in the internal affairs of the Trucial sheikhdoms as long as peace was maintained. This contrasted with their policy in Oman, where the British supported the stability of Oman's sultanate and maintained their airbase on [[Masirah Island]]. According to a British official,
{{blockquote|They could fight each other as much as they liked by land, and we did not hesitate to recognize a ruler who had acquired power by murder. The construction of an airport at Sharjah and the grant of oil concessions to an oil company forced us to modify this policy to some extent.<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://mepc.org/journal/uae-and-oman-opportunities-and-challenges-twenty-first-century|title=The UAE and Oman: Opportunities and Challenges in the Twenty-First Century|last=Al Sayegh|first=Fatma|date=2002|website=Middle East Policy Council|volume=XI|issue=3|access-date=2020-04-13|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205181426/https://mepc.org/journal/uae-and-oman-opportunities-and-challenges-twenty-first-century|url-status=live}}</ref>}}

== The decline of pearling ==
Pearling was a key economic activity throughout the C18-C19th and central to the area's coastal economy until its decline in the early C20th.<ref name=":0" /> It was key to the influence enjoyed by the Trucial Rulers: some 80 percent of [[Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan|Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan]]'s revenue was derived from the pearling fleet, giving him great influence among the tribes and allowing him to distribute largesse.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=C. Wilkinson |first1=John |date=June 2009 |title=From Liwa to Abu Dhabi |url=https://www.na.ae/en/pdfviewer.aspx?path=%2Fen%2FImages%2FLIWA01.pdf |url-status=live |journal=Liwa |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=9–10 |issn=1729-9039 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220517070810/https://www.na.ae/en/pdfviewer.aspx?path=%2Fen%2FImages%2FLIWA01.pdf |archive-date=17 May 2022 |access-date=11 April 2019}}</ref>

At the start of the pearling season, which lasted from June to September, thousands of ships gathered with provisions for three months and a starting day would be set.<ref name=":0" /> On that day, celebrations were held and religious rites observed; this included the traditional charming of sharks so they would not harm the divers. The ships then dispersed on a clear, windless day, when the sea was calm.<ref name="Fer" />

Each ship carried divers, who dove to the sea floor to gather pearls. To reach the bottom, two heavy stones were tied to the diver's feet and a cord to his waist; the cord was held by haulers who would pull the divers up.<ref name="Fer" />

At the height of the pearling industry, in the early 1900s, about 4,500 pearl boats operated from Gulf ports, employing over 74,000 people in 1907.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Lorimer |first=John |title=Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf |publisher=British Government, Bombay |year=1915 |pages=2220}}</ref> 

The initial explanation of the decline of the Gulf pearling industry and the economy of the [[Trucial States|Trucial Coast]], derived from two entries in the 1929 and 1930 British Residency Monthly Report by Hugh Biscoe (a newly-arrived administrator with experience in India and none in the Gulf), cited two factors: the invention of the [[cultured pearl]] by Japanese entrepreneur [[Kokichi Mikimoto]] and the [[Great Depression]] of 1929. Contemporary research indicates that overfishing, regional and world wars, poor weather and  mounting debt sent the industry into decline about 20 years before Biscoe's memo, with reports of consistently poor harvests and depressed markets since 1911. The industry was already beyond recovery by the time of Biscoe's reports; the Great Depression and cultured pearls played no role in its decline.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of The Seven Sands: The Human History of the United Arab Emirates |date=2025 |publisher=Motivate Media Group |isbn=978-1-86063-512-0 |location=Dubai |pages=292–3}}</ref> The Japanese cultured pearl, initially regarded as a wonder and displayed at expositions,<ref>{{Cite news |title=Mikimoto History {{!}} About Mikimoto |url=https://www.mikimoto.com/en/about-us/history.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180722125747/https://www.mikimoto.com/en/about-us/history.html |archive-date=2018-07-22 |access-date=2018-09-24 |work=Mikimoto.com |language=en}}</ref> began to be produced in commercial quantities during the late 1920s. However, the damage had already been done: in 1907, 335 pearling boats operated out of Dubai; in 1929, only 60 boats remained in port during the season.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Wilson |first=Graeme |title=Father of Dubai |publisher=Media Prima |year=1999 |pages=40–41}}</ref>

The complex system of finance that underpinned the pearling industry and the relationships among owners, pearl merchants, ''[[nakhuda]]s'' (captains), divers and pullers fell apart, leaving a large number of men unemployed.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Heard-Bey |first=Frauke |title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: a society in transition |date=2005 |publisher=Motivate |isbn=1860631673 |location=London |pages=250 |oclc=64689681}}</ref> The pearling industry [[slavery in the Trucial States|used slave labour]]; a record number of slaves approached the British Agent seeking [[manumission]] during the 1930s, reflecting the parlous state of the pearling fleet and its owners.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Heard-Bey |first=Frauke |title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: a society in transition |date=2005 |publisher=Motivate |isbn=1860631673 |location=London |pages=251 |oclc=64689681}}</ref>

== Discovery of oil ==
During the 1930s, the first [[Petroleum industry|oil company]] teams carried out preliminary surveys. An onshore concession was granted to Petroleum Development (Trucial Coast) in 1939, and an offshore concession to D'Arcy Exploration Ltd in 1952.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Morton|first1=Michael Quentin|title=The Search for Offshore Oil Abu Dhabi|journal=AAPG Explorer|date=July 2016|url=https://www.academia.edu/26765895|access-date=17 November 2016|archive-date=19 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230219202705/https://www.academia.edu/26765895|url-status=live}}</ref> Exploration concessions were limited to British companies in accordance with agreements between the trucial sheikhs and British government. Management of the Trucial Coast moved from the British government in Bombay to the Foreign Office in London in 1947 with Indian independence. The [[Political Resident]] in the Gulf headed the small team responsible for liaison with the trucial sheikhs; he was based in [[Bushehr|Bushire]] until 1946, when his office was moved to [[Bahrain]]. Day-to-day administration was carried out by the Native Agent, a post established with the 1820 treaty and abolished in 1949. This agent was bolstered by a British political officer, based in Sharjah, in 1937.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Britain and the administration of the Trucial States 1947-1965|author=Alhammadi, Muna M.|others=Markaz al-Imārāt lil-Dirāsāt wa-al-Buḥūth al-Istirātījīyah.|isbn=9789948146384|location=Abu Dhabi|pages=34–38|oclc=884280680}}</ref>

Oil was discovered under Umm Shaif (an old pearling bed in the Persian Gulf) in 1958, and in the desert at Murban in 1960. The first cargo of crude was exported from Jabel Dhanna in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi in 1962. As the emirate's oil revenue increased, Sheikh [[Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan]] built schools, housing, hospitals and roads. When Dubai began exporting oil in 1969, Sheikh [[Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum]] also used oil revenue to improve his people's quality of life.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-middle-east-14703998 |title=Middle East | Country profiles | Country profile: United Arab Emirates |work=BBC News |date=2009-03-11 |access-date=2009-07-15 |archive-date=2009-06-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090626212416/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/middle_east/country_profiles/737620.stm |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== World War II and aftermath ===
British administrators in the Trucial States were particularly sensitive to criticism during the early part of the war, particularly into 1940 when things were going badly in Europe; an Indian trading company’s agent was deported to India for expressing pro-German sentiment that year.<ref name="McNabb-2025a">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=324–332}}</ref> The ruler of Sharjah turned up the volume of his radio and played German Arabic-language broadcasts for the benefit of an increasingly-large crowd gathering nightly to hear the broadcasts, a practice which was stopped by the horrified British Residency Agent.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" /> Rumours in the [[Bazaar|souk]] about British and French failure were found to have been started by Abdullah bin Faris, the ruler's secretary, and a war of words followed between Faris' supporters and British authorities; Faris was "kept under observation".<ref name="McNabb-2025a" /> British military activity increased at the aerodrome and RAF station at Sharjah, at the RAF landing strip and refuelling depot on [[Sir Bani Yas|Sir Bani Yas Island]] in Abu Dhabi, and at smaller facilities in [[Kalba]] and Ras Al Khaimah.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=325}}</ref>

A [[Handley Page H.P.42|Handley Page HP42]] biplane airline en route from India to Sharjah in the [[Gulf of Oman]] was lost in March 1940, and a [[Vickers Wellington|Wellington]] bomber was lost in February 1943 at [[Dhadna]] in Fujairah. A monument to the crash and the death of the Wellington’s navigator stands at Dhadna, on the east coast.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Wam |date=2018-02-14 |title=Dhadnah event marks 75th anniversary of World War Two crash — News — Emirates24{{!}}7 |url=https://www.emirates247.com/news/dhadnah-event-marks-75th-anniversary-of-world-war-two-crash-2018-02-14-1.665457 |access-date=2025-07-01 |website=www.emirates247.com |language=en}}</ref> Three [[Bristol Blenheim|Blenheim]] light bombers crashed in 1942 and 1943; one was due to engine failure in Umm Al Quwain, and one ditched in shallow water off Sharjah. One was fatal, with the loss of the pilot and two crew members of a Blenheim that lost an engine as it took off from Sharjah over the desert on 1 February 1942.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

The war at sea included the August 1944 sinking off the coast of Oman by the [[Germany|German]] [[U-boat]] ''[[German submarine U-859|U859]]'' of the American [[Liberty ship|Liberty Ship]] ''[[SS John Barry|John Barry]]'', which was carrying silver ingots destined for [[Russia]]. The first sinking of a submarine in the area was the 1940 attack on the Italian submarine ''Luigi Galvani'', with the loss of 26 of its crew, after papers relating to its voyage were taken from the surrender of another Italian submarine. Thirty-one crew members were picked up by British ships. The German U-Boat ''[[German submarine U-533|U533]]'' was sunk off the coast of Fujairah by a Blenheim flying out of Sharjah. A single survivor from the U-boat was interned in Sharjah for the duration of the war.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

During the war, rationing and identity cards were introduced; commodities such as tea and sugar became valuable rarities. Opportunistic traders in Dubai smuggled and traded contraband and engaged in [[Arms trafficking|gunrunning]]. Outbreaks of disease were frequent, including outbreaks of [[cholera]]. Swarms of [[locust]]s{{snd}}considered edible by the locals{{snd}}were frequent, although a British eradication program (involving celebrated explorer [[Wilfred Thesiger]]) was successful.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

An internal conflict broke out in the Trucial States when Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum led a party of men to build a pair of watchtowers overlooking the entrance to the creek in the coastal area of Khor Ghanadah, south of Dubai, on 17 October 1945. A combination of launches and landward forces was used, with about 300 men descending on the area and planting Dubai flags. Seeing the potential of a conflict with Abu Dhabi (which also claimed Ghanadah), the British noted Rashid's breach of the 1853 Perpetual Maritime Treaty and he withdrew.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

Sheikh Shakhbut of Abu Dhabi was furious at the incursion, and camel-raiding by Bedouin tribes loyal to Abu Dhabi started to take place. Sheikh Rashid began to retaliate and launched a raid against Abu Dhabi in January 1946, capturing a large number of camels.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" /> Measures were taken by the British to force Rashid to stand down, including stopping [[British India Steam Navigation Company]] steamers from calling into Dubai on 8 June 1946. The British ruled that Ghanadah belonged to Abu Dhabi, and Dubai agreed to restitution and the return of a number of Abu Dhabi's camels on 15 July 1946.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

The signing of a treaty in March 1947 was followed by further raiding by parties from the [[Manasir (tribe)|Manasir]], [[Al Bu Shamis]] and [[Awamir]] tribes after Shakhbut failed to pay his Bedouins.
