Raiders had taken over fifty camels from Dubai by July 1947, and in August and September 300 more camels were taken by raiders and two Dubai men were killed. Abu Dhabi was blockaded by the British, resulting in peace in April 1948. The British drew the line between the two emirates at Hassyan on the coast and [[Al Ashoosh]] inland (where it remains), although the border was not finally agreed until 1968.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

The mountainous area of [[Masfout]] was seized from the town's [[Na'im|Na’im]] headman, Saqr bin Sultan Al Hamouda, by Ajman ruler [[Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III|Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi]] in 1948.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" /> After a long and debilitating conflict with its neighbour, [[Hatta, United Arab Emirates|Hatta]], Masfout could not raise a force to oppose Rashid. Part of Masfout, the village of [[Sayh Mudayrah]] and the nearby community of [[Sinadil]], were subject to a border dispute with [[Oman]] which was settled with joint sovereignty until a final border settlement in 1998 placed Sinadil on the Omani side of the border.<ref name="McNabb-2025a" />

=== Buraimi dispute ===
{{main|Buraimi dispute}}

{{See also|Trucial Oman Scouts}}

A group of about 80 Saudi Arabian guards{{snd}}40 of whom were armed{{snd}}led by the Saudi [[Emir]] of [[Ras Tanura]], Turki Abdullah al Otaishan, crossed Abu Dhabi in 1952, occupied [[Hamasa]] (one of three Omani villages in the oasis), and claimed it as part of the [[Eastern Province, Saudi Arabia|Eastern Province]] of Saudi Arabia. The sulṭan of Muscat and imam of Oman gathered their forces to expel the Saudis, but were persuaded by the British government to exercise restraint and attempts to settle the dispute by arbitration. The British military presence increased, leading to the implementation of a standstill agreement and referral of the dispute to an international arbitration tribunal. Arbitration began in Geneva in 1955, but collapsed when British arbitrator [[Reader Bullard]] objected to Saudi Arabian attempts to influence the tribunal and withdrew. A few weeks later, the Saudi party was driven from Hamasa by the [[Trucial Oman Scouts|Trucial Oman Levies]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Trucial States|first=Donald|last=Hawley|date=1970|publisher=Allen & Unwin|isbn=978-0049530058|location=London|oclc=152680}}</ref> The dispute was finally settled in 1974 by the [[Treaty of Jeddah (1974)|Treaty of Jeddah]] between UAE President Sheikh Zayed and King Faisal of Saudi Arabia.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Buraimi : the Struggle for Power, Influence and Oil in Arabia|first=Michael Quentin|last=Morton|date=2013|publisher=I.B. Tauris|isbn=978-1299908499|location=London|oclc=858974407}}</ref>

=== Trucial States Council ===
The British instituted the [[Federal Supreme Council|Trucial States Council]] in 1952 and allocated a Trucial States Development Budget, a limited fund which did little to bolster the resources of the Trucial States' rulers. Abu Dhabi did not strike oil until 1956, and revenue from oil-exploration concessions formed much of the rulers' income. When the [[Arab League]] approached them with an offer of a significant development fund, it found a receptive audience.<ref name="McNabb-2025b">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=355–357}}</ref>

An Arab League delegation headed by Egyptian diplomat and Arab League Secretary-General [[Mohamed Abdul Khalek Hassouna|Abdel Khaleq Hassouna]] visited the Trucial States on an October 1964 "mission of brotherhood", proposing the creation of a £5 million development fund for the states.<ref name="McNabb-2025b" /> [[Ras Al Khaimah]] ruler [[Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi]] and Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi of Sharjah, an ardent [[Arab nationalism|Arab nationalist]], supported the opening of an Arab League office in [[Sharjah]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Davidson |first1=Christopher M. |title=The United Arab Emirates: A Study in Survival |publisher=Lynne Rienner Publishers |year=2005 |isbn=158826274X}}</ref><ref name="McNabb-2025b" />

A wave of demonstrations broke out in the streets of the Trucial States, with increasing anti-British sentiment. Long maintaining "British prestige" on the Trucial Coast, British administrators were alarmed at the strength of the sentiment and its source: [[Nasserism]] and its [[Soviet Union|Soviet]] backers.<ref name="McNabb-2025b" /> British officials petitioned the rulers to turn down the Arab League offer, citing previous treaties in which the trucial rulers pledged not to deal with any foreign government other than the British. Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi was obstinate, even when British officials threatened to close his airspace and shut down Sharjah's power station.<ref name="McNabb-2025b" /> The British increased funding to the Trucial States Development Fund until it stood at £2.5 million, but the rulers of the Northern States were not impressed; the ruler of [[Ajman]], [[Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III|Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi]], said that "5 million pounds will go further than 2.5 million pounds".<ref name="McNabb-2025b" />

[[Terence Clark]], deputy to the ill British Political Agent in Dubai [[Glencairn Balfour Paul]], deposed Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi in a [[1965 Sharjawi coup d'état|1965 bloodless palace coup]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ulrichsen |first1=Kristian |title=The United Arab Emirates: Power, Politics and Policy-Making |date=December 2016 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1317603108 |page=47}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Balfour-Paul |first=Glen |title=Bagpipes in Babylon: a lifetime in the Arab world and beyond |date=2006 |publisher=Tauris |isbn=978-1-84511-151-9 |location=London |pages=199}}</ref> The Trucial Oman Scouts overran [[Sharjah Fort]] and removed Saqr’s brother, Abdullah bin Sultan Al Qasimi. Abdullah was accompanied by the son of Ras Al Khaimah ruler Khalid bin Saqr bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.<ref name="McNabb-2025c">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=355–357}}</ref>

Saqr was exiled to [[Bahrain]] and, eventually, to [[Cairo]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=De Butts |first=Freddie |title=Now the Dust has Settled |publisher=Tabb House |year=1995 |isbn=1873951132}}</ref> His cousin, [[Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi]] succeeded him as ruler of Sharjah on 25 June 1965.<ref name="McNabb-2025c" /><span class="anchor" id="The Dara incident"></span>
== Independence and union: 1966–1971 ==
By 1966, the British government had concluded that it could no longer afford to govern the Trucial States.<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates : a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=336|oclc=64689681}}</ref> Deliberation took place in [[Parliament of the United Kingdom|Parliament]], with a number of MPs arguing that the [[Royal Navy]] would not be able to defend the trucial sheikhdoms. UK [[Secretary of State for Defence]]  Denis Healey reported that the [[British Armed Forces]] were severely overextended and, in some respects, dangerously under-equipped to defend the sheikhdoms.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/sun-sets-on-british-empire-as-uae-raises-its-flag-1.574721|title=Sun sets on British Empire as UAE raises its flag|work=The National|access-date=2018-10-10|language=en|archive-date=2018-11-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181106231105/https://www.thenational.ae/uae/sun-sets-on-british-empire-as-uae-raises-its-flag-1.574721|url-status=live}}</ref> 

On 16 January 1968, British Prime Minister [[Harold Wilson]] announced the decision to end the treaty relationships with the seven trucial sheikhdoms which had been (with Bahrain and [[Qatar]]), under British protection.<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates : a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=337|oclc=64689681}}</ref> The British decision to withdraw was reaffirmed in March 1971 by Prime Minister [[Edward Heath]].<ref name="Morton2016" />

The region faced a host of serious local and regional problems. There were Iranian claims for Bahrain and other islands in the Gulf, territorial disputes between Qatar and Bahrain over [[Zubarah]] and the [[Hawar Islands]], and the Buraimi dispute was still unresolved by Saudi Arabia, Abu Dhabi and Oman. Views differ about the Shah of Iran's intentions; Abdullah Omran Taryam says that Iran was contemplating the occupation of Bahrain and other islands in the Gulf, and Alvandi Roham writes that the Shah had no intention of using force to resolve the Bahrain question and unsuccessfully sought a "package deal" with Great Britain for the [[Greater and Lesser Tunbs|Tunb Islands]] and [[Abu Musa]].<ref name="Taryam-2019">{{cite book|last=Taryam|first=Abdulla Omran|date=2019|title=The Establishment of the United Arab Emirates 1950-85|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781138225787|pages=75–76}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Roham |first1=Alvandi |title=Muhammad Reza Pahlavi and the Bahrain Question, 1968–1970 |journal=British Journal of Middle Eastern Studies |date=August 2010 |volume=37 |issue=2 |pages=162–163 |url=https://www.academia.edu/1946455 |access-date=2020-04-09 |archive-date=2022-04-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220418015302/https://www.academia.edu/1946455 |url-status=live }}</ref> The rulers of the emirates believed that Britain's continued presence guaranteed the region's safety, and some did not want Britain to withdraw;<ref name="Taryam-2019" /> days after the British announcement to withdraw, Sheikh Zayed tried to persuade them to honour the protection treaties by offering to pay the costs of keeping British armed forces in the Emirates. The British [[Labour Party (UK)|Labour]] government rebuffed the offer.<ref>{{cite web |author=Jonathan Gornall |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/sun-sets-on-british-empire-as-uae-raises-its-flag-1.574721/ |title=Sun sets on British Empire as UAE raises its flag — The National |date=2 December 2011 |publisher=Thenational.ae |access-date=2012-09-07 |archive-date=2017-06-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170629152914/http://www.thenational.ae/news/uae-news/sun-sets-on-british-empire-as-uae-raises-its-flag#page5 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="TEN Guide">{{cite web|url=http://guide.theemiratesnetwork.com/basics/history_of_the_emirates.php|title=History the United Arab Emirates UAE|publisher=TEN Guide|date=1972-02-11|access-date=2009-07-15|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090608021436/http://guide.theemiratesnetwork.com/basics/history_of_the_emirates.php|archive-date=2009-06-08|url-status=dead}}</ref> Dennis Healey said on the BBC TV programme ''Panorama'' that he disliked the idea of being "a sort of white slaver for Arab sheikhs", a remark for which he later apologised.<ref name="McNabb-2025h">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=359, 360}}</ref>

=== Federation of nine emirates ===
{{see also|History of Bahrain|History of Qatar}}
[[File:LocationFederationofArabEmirates.png|thumb|upright=1.25|The proposed [[Federation of Arab Emirates]], which included present-day Bahrain, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates|alt=Map of the Federation of Arab Emirates]]
[[File: Gulf emirate leaders meet to discuss union plans 1968.jpg|thumb|alt=A large group of sheikhs seated around a rectangular table|First conference on the Gulf federation in Abu Dhabi, 1968.]]

Between 8 and 11 January 1968, Labour MP [[Goronwy Roberts, Baron Goronwy-Roberts|Goronwy Roberts]] informed the trucial rulers about the British withdrawal.<ref name="McNabb-2025h" /> On 18 February, Sheikh Zayed of Abu Dhabi and Sheikh Rashid of Dubai met at [[Argoub Al Sedirah]] (a hill in the desert between their emirates) and agreed on the principle of union.<ref name="Maktoum-2012">{{Cite book|title = Spirit of the Union|last = Maktoum|first = Mohammed|publisher = Motivate|year = 2012|isbn = 9781860633300|location = UAE|pages = 30–38}}</ref> They announced their intention to form a coalition, extending an invitation to other [[Arab states of the Persian Gulf|Gulf states]] to join. Later that month, at a summit meeting attended by the rulers of Bahrain, Qatar and the Trucial States, the government of Qatar proposed the formation of a federation of Arab emirates to be governed by a council of nine rulers. The proposal was accepted, and a declaration of union was approved.<ref name="zahlan">{{cite book|last=Zahlan|first=Rosemarie Said|title=The creation of Qatar|page=104|publisher=Barnes & Noble Books|edition=print|year=1979|isbn=978-0064979658}}</ref> There was some disagreement among the rulers on matters such as the location of the capital, the drafting of a constitution and the distribution of ministries.<ref name="zahlan"/>

Further political issues surfaced when Bahrain attempted to claim a leading role in the nine-state union, and differences emerged among the rulers of the Trucial Coast, Bahrain and Qatar; the latter two were engaged in a [[Territorial disputes in the Persian Gulf|long-running dispute]] over the [[Hawar Islands]]. While Dubai's ruler, Sheikh Rashid, had a strong connection to the Qatari ruling family{{snd}}including the [[royal intermarriage]] of his daughter with the son of the Qatari emir<ref name="zahlan2">R.S. Zahlan (1979), p. 105</ref>{{snd}} the relationship between Abu Dhabi and Dubai (also cemented by intermarriage; Rashid's wife was a member of Abu Dhabi's ruling family<ref name="Maktoum-2012" />) endured the break-up of talks with Bahrain and Qatar. The sixth meeting, which took place in Abu Dhabi in October 1969, saw [[Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan]] elected as the federation's first president. There were stalemates on numerous issues during the meeting, including the position of vice-president, defense of the federation, and whether a constitution was required.<ref name="zahlan2" /> A message was read to the Supreme Council from the British Political Resident which triggered a walk-out by delegates who found it patronising,<ref name="McNabb-2025h" /> prompting Qatar to withdraw from the federation over what it perceived as foreign interference in its internal affairs.<ref>{{Cite book|title=From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates : a society in transition|last=Heard-Bey|first=Frauke|date=2005|publisher=Motivate|isbn=1860631673|location=London|pages=352–3|oclc=64689681}}</ref> This was the last meeting of the nine-member Supreme Council, and the nine-emirate federation was disbanded despite efforts by Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and Britain to reinvigorate discussions.<ref>R.S. Zahlan (1979), p. 106</ref> Bahrain became independent in August 1971, and Qatar the following month.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2011-08-16 |title=Bahrain country profile |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-middle-east-14540571 |access-date=2024-06-16 |work=BBC News |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=History of Qatar {{!}} People, Flag, Culture, & Facts {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/history-of-Qatar |access-date=2024-06-16 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref>

=== <span class="anchor" id="Declaration of the union 1971–1972"></span>Declaration of union: 1971–1972 ===
[[File:Flag-hoisting at the Union Declaration.jpg|thumb|alt=A large outdoor flag-raising ceremony|The first hoisting of the United Arab Emirates flag by the rulers of the emirates at the Union House in Dubai on 2 December 1971.]]
[[File:Meeting Room where UAE unification treaty was signed - Etihad Museum, Dubai, UAE.jpg|thumb|upright=1.2|alt=A room with a large, round table and many chairs|Meeting room where the first constitution was signed on 2 December 1971 in Dubai, now part of the [[Etihad Museum]].]]

On 29 and 30 November 1971, a contingent of the Iranian army supported by naval forces [[Seizure of Abu Musa and the Greater and Lesser Tunbs|occupied the islands of Abu Musa and the Lesser and Greater Tunbs]].<ref name=T>{{cite book |last=Taryam| first=Abdulla Omran|date=2019|title=The Establishment of the United Arab Emirates 1950-85|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781138225787|page=163}}</ref> On [[Greater Tunb]], six policemen clashed with approximately 2,000 Iranian troops; in the ensuing skirmish, four Ras Al Khaimah policemen and three Iranian soldiers were killed. The Iranian troops demolished the police station, the school, and a number of houses, forcing the natives to leave the island. The dead were buried on the island, and the residents were put on fishing boats and taken to Ras Al Khaimah.<ref name=T /><ref name=TN>{{cite news|url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/uae-s-first-martyr-remembered-1.608115|title=UAE's first martyr remembered|publisher=The National|date=6 December 2012|access-date=31 March 2020|archive-date=26 June 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190626211451/https://www.thenational.ae/uae/uae-s-first-martyr-remembered-1.608115|url-status=live}}</ref> The [[Imperial Iranian Navy]] seized the islands with little resistance from the small local Arab police force.<ref name=NYT>Schofield, Richard. Borders and territoriality in the Gulf and the Arabian peninsula during the twentieth century. In: Schofield (ed.) Territorial foundations of the Gulf states. London: UCL Press, 1994. 1-77. References on p. 38.</ref> The population of Greater Tunb in 1971 was 150.<ref>{{cite book|last=Taryam|first=Abdulla Omran|date=2019|title=The Establishment of the United Arab Emirates 1950-85|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781138225787|pages=158–159}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last=Al Qassimi|first=Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed|date=23 August 1971|title=Memorandum to Arab States on Abu Musa|publisher=The Middle East Research and Publishing Centre|location=Beirut|volume=XV|page=6-3}}</ref> The first soldier killed on the island was Salem Suhail bin Khamis, who was shot after he refused to lower the Ras Al Khaimah flag. The death of 20-year-old bin Khamis, the first martyr in the United Arab Emirates, is observed on November 30 as [[Commemoration Day]].<ref name=TN/> The ruler of [[Emirate of Sharjah|Sharjah]] was forced to agree to negotiate for Iranian troops to occupy [[Abu Musa]]. His options were to save part of the territory or permanently forego the restoration of the remaining part of the island.<ref>{{cite book|last=Taryam|first=Abdulla Omran |date=2019 |title=The Establishment of the United Arab Emirates 1950-85| publisher=Routledge |isbn=9781138225787|page=162}}</ref>

The British-Trucial States treaty was annulled on 1 December 1971, and the Trucial States became the United Arab Emirates the following day.<ref name="TEN Guide"/><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.country-data.com/cgi-bin/query/r-1021.html |title=Bahrain – INDEPENDENCE |publisher=Country-data.com |access-date=2009-07-15 |archive-date=2009-02-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090226201621/http://www.country-data.com/cgi-bin/query/r-1021.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Six former Trucial States signed the UAE's founding treaty, with a constitution quickly drafted to meet the 2 December deadline.<ref name=infoplease>{{cite web |url=http://www.infoplease.com/country/profiles/united-arab-emirates.html |title=United Arab Emirates: History, Geography, Government, and Culture |access-date=2009-07-15 |archive-date=2009-05-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090523092354/http://www.infoplease.com/country/profiles/united-arab-emirates.html |url-status=live }}</ref> On that date, at the Dubai Guesthouse (now known as Union House), the rulers of the six emirates agreed to form a union. Although the ruler of Ras Al Khaimah was present, he was not a signatory; Ras Al Khaimah joined the UAE on 10 February 1972.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=w_qCeBV9IW0C&q=trucial+states+federation&pg=PA64 |title=Britain's Revival and Fall in the Gulf: Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and the Trucial States, 1950–71 |publisher=Routledge |location=London |access-date=2009-07-15 |first=Simon C. |last=Smith |isbn=978-0-415-33192-0 |year=2004 |archive-date=2023-02-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230219202705/https://books.google.com/books?id=w_qCeBV9IW0C&q=trucial+states+federation&pg=PA64 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.encyclopedia.com/doc/1O209-TrucialOmanorTrucialStats.html |title=Trucial Oman or Trucial States – Origin of Trucial Oman or Trucial States |website=Oxford Dictionary of World Place Names |access-date=2009-07-15 |archive-date=2011-11-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111119060814/http://www.encyclopedia.com/doc/1O209-TrucialOmanorTrucialStats.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The new state was recognised first by [[Jordan]], on 2 December 1971. The UAE joined the [[Arab League]] on 6 December, and the [[United Nations]] three days later.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=364}}</ref>

== Establishment: 1972-2000 ==
The United Arab Emirates' provisional constitution established five federal bodies: the Supreme Council of Rulers; the office of the President; the Cabinet; the Federal National Council (FNC) and the federal judiciary. The constitution also allowed the ruler of each emirate to maintain sovereignty "over their own territories and territorial waters that are not within the jurisdiction of the Union". A provision of the 1971 constitution was the establishment of a new capital city for the federation to be built between Dubai and Abu Dhabi and to be called Karama (Dignity). This provision was never enacted.<ref name="McNabb-2025f">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=373}}</ref>

A program of nation-building followed; the new nation had no ministries or other formal government bodies, no national infrastructure (roads, telecom, education, and finance) or national currency. The first annual federal budget, promulgated in February 1972, envisaged spending six million [[Bahraini dinar|BD]] on social housing, electricity and communication infrastructure (including roads).<ref name="McNabb-2025f" /> 

=== Coups in Sharjah ===
The former ruler of Sharjah, Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi, and an armed group supported by Ras Al Khaimah forced his way into the palace of Sharjah ruler Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi on 24 January 1972, occupied it, and demanded to be recognized as the ruler of Sharjah.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=364–366}}</ref> The group took control of the ruler's palace around 2:30 pm, with reports of gunfire and grenade explosions in the palace. Besieged by the [[United Arab Emirates Armed Forces|Union Defence Force]] (which arrived an hour later), Saqr surrendered early on 25 January to UAE Minister of Defence [[Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum|Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum]]. Khalid was killed in the fighting.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Al Qasimi |first=Sultan bin Muhammad |title=My Early Life |publisher=Bloomsbury |year=2011 |isbn=9781408814208 |pages=285–287}}</ref>

Saqr was handed over to [[Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan|Sheikh Zayed]] by Mohammed and, according to Glencairn Balfour-Paul, "dropped in an underground hole in Buraimi".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Balfour-Paul |first=Glen |title=Bagpipes in Babylon: a lifetime in the Arab world and beyond |date=2006 |publisher=Tauris |isbn=978-1-84511-151-9 |location=London |pages=199}}</ref> According to other sources, he was tried and imprisoned until 1979 before returning to exile in Cairo.<ref name="ahr">{{cite journal |last1=Razzaq Takriti |first1=Abdel |date=June 2019 |title=Colonial Coups and the War on Popular Sovereignty |url=https://academic.oup.com/ahr/article/124/3/878/5509795?login=false |journal=[[The American Historical Review]] |volume=124 |issue=3 |pages=xii–xiv |access-date=24 December 2024}}</ref>

On 25 January 1972, the ruling family in Sharjah met to choose a new ruler for the emirate; Sheikh [[Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi]], brother of the late ruler, was unanimously chosen to succeed him. The Supreme Council met in emergency session to consider the situation and, in addition to issuing an obituary of Sheikh Khālid, proclaimed the accession.<ref>{{cite book|last=Taryam|first=Abdulla Omran| date=2019|title=The Establishment of the United Arab Emirates 1950-85| publisher=Routledge |isbn=9781138225787 |pages=169–170}}</ref> The movement to form a union took place at a time of unprecedented instability in the region, with a border dispute resulting in 22 deaths in Kalba and the January 1972 Sharjah coup; the [[Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani|emir of Qatar]] was deposed by his cousin in February 1972.<ref name="McNabb-2025i" />

On 17 June 1987, while Sultan Bin Muhammad Al Qasimi was on holiday in the UK, his elder brother Abdulaziz led a coup. Abdulaziz, faced with unanimous support for Sultan Bin Muhammad by the UAE's rulers, agreed to a reconciliation and assumed the position of crown prince while Sultan was restored as ruler.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=377–378}}</ref>

=== Tanker war ===
The newly-integrated [[United Arab Emirates Armed Forces|UAE Armed Forces]] first saw action in 1982 with the establishment of the UN-brokered [[Multinational Force in Lebanon|Multi-National Force]] in Lebanon, a peacekeeping role. UAE forces held similar peacekeeping roles in Somalia in 1992 and Kosovo in 1999.<ref name="McNabb-2025g">{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=375–377}}</ref>

For much of the 1980s, as the [[Iran–Iraq War|Iran-Iraq]] conflict and its associated [[tanker war]] in the Gulf intensified, the UAE Armed Forces were in a state of high alert. In the face of the threat of repercussions from the conflict, the GCC carried out the October 1983 Operation Peninsula Shield (its first joint military exercise). This led to the decision to create the joint [[Peninsula Shield Force]] the following year.<ref name="McNabb-2025g" />

Iran and Iraq targeted each other's oil facilities and attempted to block oil exports by attacking neutral shipping. Kuwaiti tankers tended to ship Iraqi oil, and were frequently targeted by Iran. The US re-flagged Kuwaiti oil tankers with US flags and assigned warships to escort Kuwaiti tankers through the [[Strait of Hormuz]]. The operation's first casualty, the ''Earnest Will'', was an American frigate hit by Iraqi [[Exocet]] missiles. The Iranians laid mines in the Gulf, leading to the mining of the ''[[SS Bridgeton|Bridgeton]]'' (a re-flagged Kuwaiti tanker).<ref name="McNabb-2025g" />

The escalation continued; US forces attacked [[Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps|Iranian Revolutionary Guard]] units based on oil platforms, destroying three Iranian rigs. In July 1988, the [[Iran Air Flight 655|USS ''Vincennes'']] was involved in an action against harrying Iranian gunboats. The ''Vincennes'' mistakenly targeted an Iranian Airbus A300 flying a scheduled route from Tehran to Dubai and shot it down, with the loss of all 290 passengers and crew. Two weeks later, the Iranians accepted UN Security Council Resolution 598 calling for an end to the war.<ref name="McNabb-2025g" />

==21st century==
=== <span class="anchor" id="2000–2010"></span>2000–2020 ===
[[File:Burj Khalifa.jpg|thumb|alt=A very tall skyscraper, dwarfing surrounding buildings|The [[Burj Khalifa]], the world's [[List of tallest structures|tallest structure]] and [[List of tallest buildings|building]] since its [[topping out]] in 2009.<ref name="CTBUHdb">{{cite web |url=http://skyscrapercenter.com/building/burj-khalifa/3 |title=Burj Khalifa – The Skyscraper Center |work=Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat |access-date=2020-05-19 |archive-date=2014-12-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141224151319/http://skyscrapercenter.com/building/burj-khalifa/3 |url-status=live }}</ref>]]
[[File:Hazza Al Mansouri in front of the Soyuz spacecraft simulator.jpg|thumb|alt=Hazza Al Mansouri in his astronaut suit|[[Hazza Al Mansouri]], the first Emirati astronaut, who was launched on the [[Soyuz MS-15]] spacecraft to the [[International Space Station]] on 25 September 2019.]]

After the [[September 11 attacks|9/11]] [[List of terrorist incidents|terrorist attacks]] on the United States, the UAE was identified as a [[financial centre]] used by [[Al-Qaeda]] to transfer money to the hijackers. The nation immediately cooperated with the United States, freezing accounts tied to suspected terrorists and clamping down on [[money laundering]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2006/02/23/us/us-sees-emirates-as-both-ally-and-since-911-a-foe.html|title=U.S. Sees Emirates as Both Ally and, Since 9/11, a Foe|last=Cloud|first=David S.|date=2006-02-23|work=The New York Times|access-date=2018-01-20|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=2017-11-17|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171117070615/http://www.nytimes.com/2006/02/23/us/us-sees-emirates-as-both-ally-and-since-911-a-foe.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The country had signed a military-defence agreement with the United States in 1994, and with France in 1977.<ref>FCO 8/2898, Sale of military equipment from UK to United Arab Emirates </ref>

The UAE supported [[military operation]]s from the United States and other coalition nations engaged in the invasion of Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003) and operations supporting the global [[war on terrorism]] for the [[Horn of Africa]] at [[Al Dhafra Air Base]], outside Abu Dhabi. The airbase also supported Allied operations during the [[1991 Persian Gulf War]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=McNabb |first=Alexander |title=Children of the Seven Sands |publisher=Motivate Media Group |year=2025 |isbn=9781860635120 |location=Dubai |pages=378}}</ref> and [[Operation Enduring Freedom]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Footholds for the Fighting Force |url=https://www.airandspaceforces.com/article/0206footholds/ |access-date=2025-08-13 |website=Air & Space Forces Magazine |language=en-US}}</ref>

The UAE's first president, Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, died on 2 November 2004. His eldest son, Sheikh [[Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan]], succeeded him as ruler of Abu Dhabi. In accordance with the constitution, the UAE's Supreme Council of Rulers elected Khalifa president. Sheikh [[Mohammad bin Zayed Al Nahyan]] succeeded Khalifa as [[crown prince]] of Abu Dhabi.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/middle_east/3975737.stm |title=Veteran Gulf ruler Zayed dies |work=BBC News |date=2004-11-02 |access-date=2009-07-15 |archive-date=2009-09-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090929223450/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/middle_east/3975737.stm |url-status=live }}</ref> Sheikh Maktoum bin Rashid Al Maktoum, the prime minister of the UAE and the ruler of Dubai, died in January 2006; and Crown Prince Sheikh [[Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum]] assumed both roles.<ref name="McNabb-2025i" />

In March 2006, the United States forced the state-owned [[Dubai Ports World]] to relinquish control of terminals at six major American ports. Critics of the ports deal feared an increased risk of terrorist attack, saying that the UAE was home to two of the 9/11 hijackers.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-middle-east-14703998 | work=BBC News | title=United Arab Emirates profile | date=2013-10-26 | access-date=2018-07-21 | archive-date=2020-11-27 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201127014200/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-middle-east-14703998 | url-status=live }}</ref>

In December 2006, the UAE prepared for its first election to determine half the members of UAE's [[Federal National Council]] from 450 candidates. Only 7,000 Emirati citizens (less than one percent of the Emirati population) had the right to vote in the election; the manner of selection was opaque, but women were included in the electorate.<ref>{{cite news| last= Wheeler |first=Julia |date=15 December 2006 |title=UAE prepares for first elections|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/middle_east/6182581.stm|access-date=17 July 2015| url-status= live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120413153233/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/middle_east/6182581.stm |archive-date= 13 April 2012}}</ref>

In August 2011, the Middle East experienced a number of pro-democratic uprisings popularly known as the [[Arab Spring]]. The UAE had comparatively little unrest but in a high-profile case, five political activists were arrested for [[defamation]] by insulting heads of state (UAE president Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan, vice president Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, and Abu Dhabi crown prince Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan) on an anti-government website.<ref name=AI1>{{cite web |url=https://www.amnesty.org/en/news-and-updates/uae-end-trial-activists-charged-insulting-officials-2011-07-17 |title=UAE: End Trial of Activists Charged with Insulting Officials |date=17 July 2011 |publisher=[[Amnesty International]] |access-date=22 January 2012 |archive-date=8 December 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141208084542/http://www.amnesty.org/en/news-and-updates/uae-end-trial-activists-charged-insulting-officials-2011-07-17 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The trial of the [[UAE Five]] attracted international publicity and protest from a number of human-rights groups,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-middle-east-15922492 |title=Five jailed UAE activists 'receive presidential pardon{{'-}} |date=28 November 2011 |work=BBC News |access-date=22 January 2012 |archive-date=9 April 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190409062932/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-middle-east-15922492 |url-status=live }}</ref> including [[Amnesty International]], which called the five men [[prisoners of conscience]].<ref name=AI1 /> The defendants were convicted and given two- to three-year prison sentences on 27 November 2011.<ref name="AJ">{{cite web |date=28 November 2011 |title=UAE pardons jailed activists |url=http://www.aljazeera.com/news/middleeast/2011/11/20111128135953601809.html |access-date=22 January 2012 |publisher=Al Jazeera}}</ref>

On 25 September 2019, [[Hazza Al Mansouri]] flew aboard the [[Soyuz MS-15]] spacecraft to the [[International Space Station]], where he stayed for eight days as the first Emirati in space.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2019-09-21 |title=UAE in space: Hazzaa Al Mansoori to become first Emirati astronaut |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-middle-east-49715269 |access-date=2023-09-02}}</ref>

===<span class="anchor" id="2020-present"></span>Since 2020===
[[File:Mars - August 30 2021 - Flickr - Kevin M. Gill.png|thumb|alt=See caption|Photo of Mars taken by the [[Emirates Mars Mission]]'s Hope probe.]]

The first confirmed [[COVID-19]] case in the [[United Arab Emirates]] was announced on 29 January 2020. It was the first country in the [[Middle East]] to report a confirmed case.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Turak |first=Natasha |date=2020-01-29 |title=First Middle East cases of coronavirus confirmed in the UAE |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/01/29/first-middle-east-cases-of-coronavirus-confirmed-in-the-uae.html |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=CNBC |language=en}}</ref>

The United Arab Emirates launched the [[Emirates Mars Mission]] that year, a [[United Arab Emirates Space Agency]] [[uncrewed]] [[space exploration]] mission to [[Mars]]. The Hope probe was launched on 19 July 2020, and went into orbit around Mars on 9 February 2021.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Live coverage: Emirates Mars Mission launches from Japan – Spaceflight Now |url=https://spaceflightnow.com/2020/07/19/h-2a-emirates-mars-mission-mission-status-center-2/ |access-date=2023-09-02 |language=en-US}}</ref> The United Arab Emirates became the first Arab country and the fifth country to reach Mars, and the second country to enter Mars' orbit on its first try (after India). In April 2023, ''[[The New York Times]]'' reported an updated global map of Mars based on images from the Hope spacecraft.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Chang |first=Kenneth |date=2023-04-15 |title=New Mars Map Lets You 'See the Whole Planet at Once' |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/04/15/science/astronomy-mars-atlas.html |access-date=2023-09-02 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref>

During the early 2020s, the UAE began overhauling its criminal and civil laws. It legalized alcohol, ended lighter punishments for [[honor killings]], enacted harsher punishments for [[rape]] and [[sexual harassment]], allowed foreigners to follow their home-country's family laws for marriage and inheritance rather than [[Sharia]], reduced penalties for drugs and having a child when unmarried, and allowed unmarried couples to live together. Economic changes have allowed foreigners to own businesses without a UAE partner. The UAE moved to a Saturday-Sunday [[weekend]] at the beginning of 2022, with Friday a working half-day.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Zakir |first=SM Ayaz |title=UAE: Remember when weekends used to be Friday-Saturday? How residents' lifestyles changed |url=https://www.khaleejtimes.com/life-and-living/uae-weekend-change-how-1-year-of-saturday-sunday-off-impacted-residents-businesses |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=Khaleej Times |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/nov/07/united-arab-emirates-to-relax-islamic-laws-on-personal-freedoms |title=UAE decriminalises alcohol and lifts ban on unmarried couples living together |website=[[TheGuardian.com]] |date=9 November 2020 |access-date=2022-01-11 |archive-date=2022-01-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220111155359/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/nov/07/united-arab-emirates-to-relax-islamic-laws-on-personal-freedoms |url-status=live |last1=Graham-Harrison |first1=Emma }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last1=Sherlock |first1=Ruth |url=https://www.npr.org/2022/01/10/1071896627/the-uae-attempts-to-overhaul-harsh-criminal-and-financial-laws |title=The UAE attempts to overhaul harsh criminal and financial laws |website=[[NPR]] |date=10 January 2022 |access-date=2022-01-11 |archive-date=2022-01-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220111142918/https://www.npr.org/2022/01/10/1071896627/the-uae-attempts-to-overhaul-harsh-criminal-and-financial-laws |url-status=live }}</ref> Homosexuality remained illegal,<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2021/06/30/us-and-uk-embassies-in-uae-see-backlash-for-flying-gay-pride-flag.html |title=U.S. and UK embassies in the UAE face backlash after flying rainbow pride flag in historic regional first |website=[[CNBC]] |date=30 June 2021 |access-date=2022-01-11 |archive-date=2021-07-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210704070626/https://www.cnbc.com/2021/06/30/us-and-uk-embassies-in-uae-see-backlash-for-flying-gay-pride-flag.html |url-status=live }}</ref> although a new 21+ age rating allowed uncensored movies to be shown.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://variety.com/2021/film/news/united-arab-emirates-film-censorship-1235139554/ |title=United Arab Emirates Moves Needle Forward Against Film Censorship With New Rating Category |date=20 December 2021 |access-date=2022-01-11 |archive-date=2022-01-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220111161257/https://variety.com/2021/film/news/united-arab-emirates-film-censorship-1235139554/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

[[Expo 2020]] was a [[World Expo]] hosted by Dubai from 1 October 2021 to 31 March 2022. Originally scheduled for 20 October 2020 to 10 April 2021, it was postponed due to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United Arab Emirates]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Abbas |first=Waheed |title=Dubai Expo confirms new dates: Oct 1, 2021 until Mar 31, 2022 |url=https://www.khaleejtimes.com/expo/dubai-expo-confirms-new-dates-oct-1-2021-until-mar-31-2022 |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=Khaleej Times |language=en}}</ref> Despite the postponement, organizers kept the name Expo 2020 for [[marketing]] and [[brand]]ing purposes. The event recorded more than 24 million visits in its six months.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Halligan |first=Neil |date=2022-04-02 |title=Expo 2020 Dubai records more than 24 million visits after late surge |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/expo-2020/2022/04/02/expo-2020-dubai-records-more-than-24-million-visits-after-late-surge-in-numbers/ |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=The National |language=en}}</ref>

On 22 February 2022, the [[Museum of the Future]] was opened by the [[Federal government of the United Arab Emirates|government]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Reporter |first=A. Staff |title=Dubai Museum of the Future opens today: 7 experiences you will find inside |url=https://www.khaleejtimes.com/uae-attractions/dubai-museum-of-the-future-opens-tomorrow-7-experiences-you-will-find-inside |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=Khaleej Times |language=en}}</ref> The date was chosen because it is a [[palindrome]] date.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-02-22 |title=Dubai opens new Museum of the Future |url=https://nypost.com/2022/02/22/dubai-opens-new-museum-of-the-future/ |access-date=2023-09-02 |language=en-US}}</ref> The museum is devoted to Dubai's innovative and futuristic technologies.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-03-30 |title=Calligraphy-covered Museum of the Future nears completion in Dubai |url=https://www.dezeen.com/2021/03/30/museum-of-the-future-dubai-killa-design-buro-happold-arabic-calligraphy/ |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=Dezeen |language=en}}</ref> On 14 May 2022, Sheikh [[Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan]] was elected as UAE president after the death of Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan.<ref>{{cite news |title=Who is MBZ, the UAE's new president? |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/5/14/who-is-sheikh-mohammed-bin-zayed-al-nahyan-mbz-uaes-new-president |work=www.aljazeera.com |language=en |access-date=2022-05-16 |archive-date=2022-05-16 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220516064657/https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/5/14/who-is-sheikh-mohammed-bin-zayed-al-nahyan-mbz-uaes-new-president |url-status=live }}</ref>

The [[Emirates Lunar Mission]] was the first mission to the [[Moon]] from the UAE.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-09-29 |title=UAE to launch new Emirati space mission to explore moon: Dubai ruler |url=https://english.alarabiya.net/News/gulf/2020/09/29/UAE-to-launch-new-Emirati-space-mission-to-explore-moon-Dubai-ruler |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=Al Arabiya English |language=en}}</ref> The mission by [[Mohammed bin Rashid Space Centre]] (MBRSC) sent a [[lunar rover]] named Rashid to the Moon aboard [[Ispace (Japanese company)|ispace]]'s [[Hakuto-R Mission 1]] lander.<ref name="Rosenstein">{{Cite web |last=Rosenstein |first=Sawyer |date=2022-12-11 |title=SpaceX launches Falcon 9 carrying private Japanese moon lander |url=https://www.nasaspaceflight.com/2022/12/hakuto-r-m1/ |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=NASASpaceFlight.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=ispace、UAEドバイ政府宇宙機関MBRSCとペイロード輸送契約を締結 |url=https://ispace-inc.com/jpn/news/?p=1874 |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=ispace |language=ja}}</ref> Launched on 11 December 2022 on a [[Falcon 9 Block 5]] rocket,<ref name="Rosenstein"/> the rover attempted to land in the [[Atlas (crater)|Atlas crater]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=ispace Announces Mission 1 Launch Date |url=https://ispace-inc.com/news-en/?p=3939 |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=ispace |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Victor">{{Cite news |last=Victor |first=Daniel |date=2023-04-25 |title=Moon Landing Updates: What Happened During Ispace's Moon Landing Attempt |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/live/2023/04/25/science/ispace-moon-landing-japan |access-date=2023-09-02 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> On 25 April 2023, seconds before the attempted landing, communication with the Hakuto-R lander was lost.<ref name="Victor"/> The ispace team confirmed that the spacecraft had crashed into the Moon and was destroyed.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Status Update on ispace HAKUTO-R Mission 1 Lunar Lander |url=https://ispace-inc.com/news-en/?p=4655 |access-date=2023-09-02 |website=ispace |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2023-04-25 === The UAE and the 2026 Iran War ===
Following [[Operation Epic Fury]], Iran launched missiles to [[Israel]] and [[Arab states of the Persian Gulf|Gulf States]], [[2026 Iranian strikes on the United Arab Emirates|Including the UAE]], damage was reported in [[Al Dhafra Air Base|Al-Dhafra Airbase]], [[Fairmont The Palm|Fairmont the Palm Hotel]], [[Burj Al Arab|Burj Al-Arab]], and [[Dubai International Airport]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Staff |first=Al Jazeera |title=Multiple Arab states that host US assets targeted in Iran retaliation |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/28/multiple-gulf-arab-states-that-host-us-assets-targeted-in-iran-retaliation |access-date=2026-04-19 |website=Al Jazeera |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Christou |first=William |last2=Giuffrida |first2=Angela |date=2026-03-01 |title=Explosions rock Dubai, Bahrain, Jordan and Kuwait as war spreads across Middle East |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/28/dubais-famous-fairmont-hotel-in-flames-after-iranian-air-strike |access-date=2026-04-19 |work=The Guardian |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>
